units.title: Processes & Threads
units.cold_probe: A shell runs ls. Walk through fork, exec, and wait: what does fork return in each process, why does exec not return on success, and what happens to the child's kernel-side state if the parent never calls wait?
units.brief:
A process is a program in execution: a private virtual address space (text, data/bss, heap, stack), open file descriptors, credentials, signal handlers, and at least one thread of execution. A thread is the schedulable unit inside a process: it owns its registers (PC, stack pointer), its stack, errno and TLS, while sharing the address space, heap, globals, file descriptor table, and signal dispositions with sibling threads. The interview one-liner: processes are the unit of isolation and resource ownership; threads are the unit of scheduling and concurrency.

The kernel tracks each process in a Process Control Block (PCB; task_struct on Linux): PID, state (new/ready/running/waiting/terminated), saved CPU registers, kernel stack pointer, scheduling info (priority, vruntime), memory-management info (pointer to the page tables / mm_struct), open-file table, pending signals, accounting, and parent/child links. On a context switch the kernel saves the running task's registers into its PCB and restores the next task's.

- Direct switch cost: save/restore registers, swap kernel stacks - hundreds of ns to a few microseconds.
- Indirect cost dominates: switching address spaces reloads the page-table base register (CR3 on x86), which historically flushed the TLB (mitigated by ASIDs/PCID), and the incoming task runs on cold caches and branch predictors. This is why thread switches within a process are cheaper - no address-space switch - and why user-level switches are cheaper still.

User-level vs kernel-level threads. Kernel threads (1:1 - what Linux pthreads and C++ std::thread use) are visible to the scheduler: they run in parallel on multiple cores, and one blocking in a syscall does not block siblings; but creation and switching cross into the kernel. User-level threads (M:1, green threads) are scheduled by a runtime library: switches are cheap function calls, but the kernel sees one thread - a blocking syscall blocks them all and there is no true parallelism. M:N hybrids (Go goroutines over a small pool of kernel threads) chase both; the runtime must intercept blocking calls to make it work.

The Unix creation model is fork/exec/wait:
- fork() clones the caller: the child gets a copy of the address space (copy-on-write in practice - pages shared read-only, copied on first write), a copy of the file descriptor table (descriptors reference the same open-file entries, so file offsets are shared), and the same program counter. It returns twice: the child's PID in the parent, 0 in the child, -1 on failure.
- exec() replaces the current address space with a new program; on success it never returns. The PID and open fds (minus O_CLOEXEC ones) survive. fork+exec is how the shell launches jobs; the gap between the two calls is where redirection (dup2) and pipe wiring happen - that separation is exactly why Unix has two calls instead of one spawn.
- wait()/waitpid() lets the parent collect the child's exit status.

Zombies and orphans. When a child exits, the kernel must keep its exit status until the parent reads it, so a stub of the PCB (PID + status) lingers: that is a zombie (state Z, shown as defunct). A zombie holds no memory beyond the PCB but consumes a PID; a server that forks and never waits eventually exhausts the PID space. Fixes: wait/waitpid (commonly a SIGCHLD handler looping waitpid with WNOHANG), setting SIGCHLD to SIG_IGN, or the double-fork trick so the grandchild is reparented. An orphan is the mirror case - the parent dies first; the child is reparented to init/PID 1 (or a subreaper), which dutifully waits, so orphans do not become permanent zombies. You cannot kill a zombie - it is already dead; reap it via its parent.

C++ flavor: std::thread is a 1:1 kernel thread; destroying a joinable std::thread calls std::terminate, so always join() or detach() (or use std::jthread). After fork() in a multithreaded program only the calling thread exists in the child - mutexes held by other threads stay locked forever - so the rule is: between fork and exec in a threaded program, do only async-signal-safe work.
must_hit: fork returns twice: child PID in parent, 0 in child, -1 on failure
fork is copy-on-write, not an eager memory copy
exec replaces the address space but keeps PID and open fds
wait reaps the child's exit status
no wait -> zombie: dead child's PCB/PID lingers until reaped or parent dies
misconceptions: fork copies all of memory immediately - it marks pages copy-on-write; copying happens lazily, one page at a time, on first write
a zombie is a runaway process you can kill -9 - it is already dead; only the parent's wait (or the parent dying, so init reaps it) removes it
threads have separate address spaces - threads share one address space; only registers, stack, and TLS are per-thread, and even stacks are mutually visible memory
more threads always means more speed - beyond core count you buy context-switch and cache costs, not parallelism; speedup is capped by the serial fraction (Amdahl)
rapid: fork: child PID in parent, 0 in child, -1 on failure
exec never returns on success; PID and non-CLOEXEC fds survive
Linux makes processes and threads with the same clone() syscall; flags pick what is shared
zombie = dead child not yet reaped; orphan = live child reparented to init/subreaper

std::thread destroyed while joinable calls std::terminate
after fork in a multithreaded process, only the calling thread exists in the child

units.title: CPU Scheduling
units.cold_probe: Jobs A=24ms, B=3ms, C=3ms arrive at t=0 in that order. Compute the average waiting time under FCFS and under SJF, and name the effect that hurts FCFS here.
units.brief:
Scheduling decides which ready thread gets a core next. Know the metrics by name: CPU utilization, throughput, turnaround time (submission to completion), waiting time (time spent in the ready queue), response time (submission to first output). Batch systems optimize throughput/turnaround; interactive systems optimize response time; real-time systems optimize deadline compliance. A non-preemptive scheduler switches only when the running task blocks or exits; a preemptive one can take the CPU at a timer interrupt or when a higher-priority task arrives - better response, but it costs switches and creates the shared-data races that unit 3 exists to solve.

- FCFS: run in arrival order, non-preemptive. Trivial and starvation-free, but it suffers the convoy effect: one long CPU burst at the head makes every short job wait behind it, and average waiting time becomes extremely order-sensitive (bursts 24,3,3 give (0+24+27)/3 = 17ms average wait; order 3,3,24 gives 3ms).
- SJF: run the shortest next CPU burst. Provably minimizes average waiting time - swap any adjacent inversion and the average drops (exchange argument). Two problems: burst lengths are unknown in advance, so real systems estimate them with an exponential average tau(n+1) = a*t(n) + (1-a)*tau(n); and long jobs starve under a steady stream of short ones. SRTF is the preemptive variant - a new arrival with smaller remaining time preempts - lower averages still, and even more starvation-prone.
- RR: FCFS plus a time quantum q; the preempted task goes to the tail. Response-friendly and fair: with n tasks, no one waits more than (n-1)q for its next slice. The classic question is tuning q: too large and RR degenerates to FCFS; too small and context-switch overhead eats the CPU. You want q much larger than the switch cost; the old rule of thumb is that about 80 percent of CPU bursts should finish within one quantum. Typical values are a few milliseconds. Note RR worsens turnaround for equal-length jobs compared to running them to completion.
- Priority: always run the highest priority, preemptively or not. Strict priorities starve the low end - fix with aging, where priority rises with waiting time. Priority also enables priority inversion: a low-priority task holds a lock a high-priority task needs, while a medium-priority task preempts the low one - the high task is effectively blocked by the medium one. This hit the Mars Pathfinder in 1997. Fixes: priority inheritance (the lock holder temporarily inherits the highest waiting priority) or priority ceiling protocols.
- MLFQ: multiple queues at descending priorities, usually with larger quanta lower down. New tasks enter at the top; using a full quantum demotes you; blocking early (interactive, IO-bound behavior) keeps you high. MLFQ learns burst behavior online - it approximates SJF without knowing burst lengths, which is why it is the answer to how real time-sharing systems schedule. Two refinements interviewers love: charge total CPU consumed at a level rather than per-quantum, so a task cannot game the system by yielding just before expiry; and periodically boost everything to the top queue, which cures starvation and adapts when a task changes phase from batch to interactive.

Linux CFS one-liner: the Completely Fair Scheduler gives each runnable task a vruntime - actual runtime weighted by its nice level - and always runs the task with the smallest vruntime, kept in a red-black tree for O(log n) selection. There is no fixed quantum; a target latency window is divided among runnable tasks with a minimum granularity floor. Wakers get their vruntime clamped near the current minimum, so interactive tasks run promptly without banking unbounded credit. Bonus point: since kernel 6.6 CFS has been replaced by EEVDF, which adds explicit latency deadlines to the fairness model. Real-time classes SCHED_FIFO and SCHED_RR sit strictly above the fair class.

When asked to design a scheduler, state the workload first (batch, interactive, real-time), name the metric that matters, then map it to a policy. Interviewers grade the goals-to-mechanism mapping, not acronym recall.
must_hit: SJF is optimal for average waiting time but needs burst knowledge and can starve long jobs
misconceptions: SJF is directly implementable - future burst lengths are unknown; systems estimate via exponential averaging or approximate SJF online with MLFQ
RR is strictly better than FCFS - RR improves response time but worsens turnaround for similar-length jobs, and a tiny quantum drowns in switch overhead
priority scheduling just works - strict priority starves low-priority tasks and enables priority inversion; you need aging and priority inheritance
preemption is free - it adds context-switch cost and introduces synchronization races on shared state; non-preemptive run-to-completion still has its uses
rapid: SJF provably minimizes average waiting time (exchange argument)
RR bound: each task waits at most (n-1) x quantum for its next slice
quantum too big -> FCFS; too small -> switch overhead dominates
aging = priority rises with waiting time; the standard starvation cure
convoy effect: short jobs pile up behind one long burst under FCFS
MLFQ: demote on full quantum use; periodic boost to top cures starvation
CFS: run smallest vruntime, red-black tree, O(log n); nice sets the weight
priority inversion fix: priority inheritance (Mars Pathfinder is the war story)

units.title: Synchronization & Deadlock
units.cold_probe: Thread 1 locks mutex A then B; thread 2 locks B then A. Name the four conditions that make deadlock possible here, state which one the standard fix breaks, and name the C++ facility that implements that fix.
units.brief:
A race condition: the outcome depends on the interleaving of unsynchronized accesses to shared state, at least one being a write. count++ is load-modify-store: two threads can both load 5 and both store 6. In C++ this is formally a data race and undefined behavior - not merely a stale value.

A critical section is code that must appear atomic to other threads. A correct solution needs three properties: mutual exclusion; progress (if nobody is inside, entry cannot be indefinitely postponed by threads outside the section); and bounded waiting (a requester eventually enters). Software-only solutions like Peterson's algorithm work for two threads on paper but assume sequential consistency that modern hardware only gives with fences; real locks are built on atomic read-modify-write instructions: test-and-set, compare-and-swap, fetch-and-add. Disabling interrupts prevents races only inside a uniprocessor kernel.

The primitive zoo - the differences are what interviews probe:
- Mutex: a lock with ownership - only the locking thread may unlock. Sleeps when contended. Ownership is what enables priority inheritance, recursion detection, and error checking.
- Semaphore: a counter with wait (P: decrement or block) and signal (V: increment and wake). No ownership - any thread may signal - which makes it a signaling and quota primitive, not just a lock. A counting semaphore models N identical resources; a binary semaphore resembles a mutex but is not one.
- Spinlock: busy-wait on an atomic flag. No sleep, no context switch - correct when critical sections are shorter than a switch (about a microsecond), on multiprocessors, and mandatory in interrupt context where sleeping is forbidden. Pointless on a uniprocessor: spinning prevents the holder from running. Real mutexes are adaptive - spin briefly, then sleep (Linux futex).
- Monitor: a language-level construct - a lock implicitly protecting an object, plus condition variables for waiting (Java synchronized is the canonical example). A condvar wait must atomically release the mutex and sleep; otherwise a signal can arrive in the gap and be lost forever (lost wakeup). Under Mesa semantics - what every real system implements - signal merely makes a waiter runnable; by the time it runs the condition may be false again, and spurious wakeups are allowed. Therefore always: while (!pred) wait;. In C++, cv.wait(lock, pred) encodes exactly that loop.

Producer-consumer (bounded buffer): semaphores empty=N, full=0, mutex=1. Producer: wait(empty); lock(mutex); insert; unlock(mutex); signal(full). Consumer mirrors with full/empty swapped. Order matters: taking the mutex before wait(empty) deadlocks when the buffer is full - the producer sleeps holding the mutex the consumer needs to drain the buffer. Readers-writers: many concurrent readers or one exclusive writer. Reader preference starves writers under a steady reader stream; writer preference starves readers; fair versions serve arrivals in order. C++ has std::shared_mutex (shared_lock for readers, unique_lock for writers).

Deadlock: a set of threads each waiting for a resource held by another member - blocked forever, no state change. All four Coffman conditions must hold simultaneously: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait. Four strategies:
- Prevention: statically break one condition. The practical choice is a global lock acquisition order, which breaks circular wait; std::lock and std::scoped_lock acquire multiple mutexes deadlock-free. Breaking hold-and-wait (acquire everything up front, or trylock and release all on failure) works but risks livelock.
- Avoidance: stay in safe states dynamically - Banker's algorithm: with maximum claims declared in advance, grant a request only if some completion order still exists for all processes afterward. Unsafe does not mean deadlocked - it means deadlock has become possible. Rarely deployed, because real programs cannot declare maximum claims.
- Detection and recovery: let deadlock happen, periodically search the wait-for graph for cycles, then kill a victim or preempt and roll back. Databases do exactly this: abort one transaction.
- Ostrich: ignore the problem - what general-purpose OSes do for user-space locks.

Livelock: threads keep changing state but make no progress - both trylock, both fail, both release, both retry in lockstep. Randomized backoff breaks the symmetry. Starvation: the system progresses but one thread never does (writers under reader preference; low priority under strict priority) - cured by fairness, queuing, aging. Deadlock is the extreme case where nobody progresses.

C++ specifics worth dropping: std::lock_guard for RAII unlock even on exceptions; std::unique_lock (movable, required by condition_variable); std::atomic for counters and flags; double-checked locking is broken without atomics and correctly replaced by std::call_once or a C++11 magic static, whose initialization is thread-safe by the standard.
must_hit: all four Coffman conditions: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait
global lock ordering breaks circular wait
std::lock / std::scoped_lock acquire multiple mutexes deadlock-free
alternative: trylock + release-all + retry, at the cost of livelock risk
misconceptions: a mutex and a binary semaphore are the same thing - a mutex has ownership (only the locker unlocks; enables priority inheritance and error checking); a semaphore is ownerless signaling
if (cond) wait(cv) is fine - it must be a while loop: spurious wakeups exist and Mesa semantics mean the predicate can be false again by the time you run
deadlock needs a big system - two threads and two mutexes acquired in opposite order satisfy all four Coffman conditions
disabling interrupts stops races - only in a uniprocessor kernel; other cores keep executing, and user code cannot disable interrupts at all
rapid: Coffman: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait - all four necessary
lock ordering is the practical deadlock fix; it breaks circular wait
spinlock: hold time shorter than a context switch, SMP only, never while sleeping
condvar wait atomically releases the mutex and sleeps - otherwise lost wakeups
bounded buffer init: mutex=1, empty=N, full=0
reader-preference RW locks starve writers; fair versions queue arrivals
livelock = state changes, no progress; starvation = others progress, you never do
Banker's = avoidance via safe states; needs max claims declared up front

units.title: Memory Management
units.cold_probe: A load instruction misses the TLB, and the page-table walk finds the page not present. List, in order, everything the hardware and the OS do until the load finally completes.
units.brief:
Virtual memory gives each process a private address space and lets the OS overcommit physical RAM. The payoffs: isolation and protection, programs larger than RAM (demand paging), a uniform layout that simplifies linking and ASLR, cheap sharing (libraries, copy-on-write), no external fragmentation of physical memory, and memory-mapped files.

Paging: the virtual address space is split into fixed-size pages (4KB typical), physical memory into equal-size frames. A page table maps virtual page number (VPN) to physical frame number (PFN) plus permission bits: present, writable, user/supervisor, accessed, dirty, no-execute. The page offset passes through untranslated.

A flat page table is infeasible: 48-bit addresses with 4KB pages need 2^36 entries x 8 bytes = 512GB per process. Multi-level tables make the table a sparse tree: x86-64 splits the 48 bits into four 9-bit indexes (PML4 -> PDPT -> PD -> PT) plus a 12-bit offset; subtrees for unmapped regions simply do not exist, so a small process has a tiny table. The cost: one translation now takes up to 4 memory reads. The TLB - a small associative cache of recent VPN-to-PFN translations - makes this survivable: hits are essentially free; a miss triggers a hardware walk. Effective access time = h*t + (1-h)*(t + walk). Context switches historically forced a TLB flush; ASIDs/PCIDs tag entries with an address-space id so they can coexist. Huge pages (2MB, 1GB) shorten walks and multiply TLB reach (reach = entries x page size).

Page faults are the mechanism, not the disease. On access, if the PTE says not-present or permissions fail, the MMU traps to the kernel. The kernel checks its VMA list: invalid address -> SIGSEGV. Valid: a minor fault needs no disk IO - the page is already in the page cache, or it is a fresh anonymous page (map the shared zero page / allocate), or a COW copy - map it and return. A major fault schedules a disk read, blocks the thread (another runs meanwhile), and on IO completion fills the PTE and re-executes the faulting instruction - which is why hardware must support precise instruction restart. Demand paging means nothing is resident at start; pages fault in on first touch - fast startup, pay-as-you-go.

Replacement, when a free frame is needed: optimal (evict the page used farthest in the future) is the offline lower bound. LRU approximates it via temporal locality, but exact per-reference LRU bookkeeping is too expensive, so real systems use clock / second chance: sweep a hand over frames clearing hardware reference bits, evict the first page found unreferenced (Linux keeps active/inactive LRU lists; MGLRU now). FIFO is simple but evicts hot pages and exhibits Belady's anomaly - more frames can cause more faults; the classic string 1,2,3,4,1,2,5,1,2,3,4,5 gives 9 faults with 3 frames and 10 with 4. Stack algorithms (LRU, OPT) are provably immune. Dirty pages cost double (write back before reuse), so clean victims are preferred; accessed and dirty bits are set by the hardware for free.

Thrashing: when the sum of active working sets exceeds RAM, every quantum starts with faults; CPU utilization collapses while the disk saturates - and a naive scheduler sees the idle CPU and admits more processes, making it worse. The working set model W(t, delta) = the set of pages referenced in the last delta references; keep each process's working set fully resident, or swap entire processes out (medium-term scheduling) until the rest fit. The page-fault-frequency alternative: fault rate above threshold -> grant frames; below -> reclaim some. Detection signature: low CPU, high paging IO.

Segmentation vs paging: segments are variable-length, programmer-visible units (code, data, stack) with base+limit registers - natural sharing and protection, but variable sizes cause external fragmentation and force compaction. Pages are fixed-size and invisible - no external fragmentation (any frame fits any page), but internal fragmentation: on average half a page wasted per region, more with huge pages. Modern reality: paging does the work; x86-64 forces the CS/DS/ES/SS segment bases to zero in 64-bit mode, but FS and GS keep non-zero bases (set via MSRs) - exactly how TLS and per-CPU data are implemented; the segmentation idea survives as kernel VMAs - regions with permissions.

Copy-on-write: map the shared pages read-only in both page tables and refcount the frames; a write traps, the kernel sees the VMA is logically writable, copies that one page for the writer, updates the PTE, resumes. Powers fork (child created in O(page-table-copy) time), shared library data pages, and dedup (KSM). Classic follow-up: why is fork+immediate-exec still slightly wasteful, and what fixes it? The page tables are still copied; vfork and posix_spawn exist to skip even that.

units.title: Storage, Files & Syscalls
units.cold_probe: Trace read(fd, buf, 4096) from the C call until data is in buf: the user-to-kernel transition, the role of the page cache, and what changes when the block is not cached.
units.brief:
On-disk layout of a classic Unix filesystem (ext family): boot block, superblock (filesystem-wide metadata: sizes, free counts), inode bitmap and data bitmap, inode table, then data blocks. The inode IS the file: type, permissions, uid/gid, size, timestamps, link count, and pointers to data blocks - traditionally 12 direct pointers, then single, double, and triple indirect blocks (ext4 uses extents: contiguous runs described compactly). The filename is NOT in the inode. A directory is just a file whose contents are (name, inode number) entries. Path resolution walks component by component: read the root inode, scan its data for the next name, load that inode, repeat - which is why kernels keep a dentry cache for name lookups.

- Hard link: a second directory entry pointing at the same inode; the link count increments. rm is unlink(): data blocks are freed only when the link count reaches zero AND no process still holds the file open. An open-but-unlinked file is both a classic disk-space mystery (df says full, du disagrees) and a deliberate temp-file technique. Hard links cannot cross filesystems (inode numbers are per-filesystem) and cannot point at directories (cycle risk).
- Symbolic link: a separate small file whose data is a pathname, resolved on every traversal - it can dangle, can cross filesystems, can name a directory; costs an extra lookup.

The page cache sits under all file IO. A read() that hits the cache is a memory copy, no device involved; writes normally land in the cache and reach disk later via writeback - so buffered data dies in a crash unless you fsync (databases fsync their logs; O_DIRECT bypasses the cache entirely for engines that manage their own). This cache is why free memory on Linux always looks low: cache pages are reclaimable on demand.

System call path: user code calls a libc wrapper; the wrapper puts the syscall number and arguments in registers and executes the syscall instruction (x86-64). The CPU switches to ring 0 at a fixed kernel entry point and swaps to the thread's kernel stack; the kernel dispatches through the syscall table and validates every user pointer (copy_from_user / copy_to_user) - the kernel must never dereference a user address blindly. Return via sysret. This is a mode switch, not a context switch: same task, same address space, but still roughly 50-300ns plus indirect costs (KPTI, cache disturbance). That cost is why everything batches: stdio buffering, readv/writev, epoll instead of per-fd polling, io_uring to submit many operations per crossing, and the vDSO so that clock_gettime never enters the kernel at all.

Interrupts: the alternative to burning CPU on polling. A device raises an IRQ; the CPU finishes the current instruction, saves minimal state, and vectors through the interrupt descriptor table to a handler running in interrupt context - where sleeping is forbidden. Linux splits the work: the top half acknowledges the device and queues work with the IRQ masked, staying minimal; the bottom half (softirq, tasklet, or workqueue) does the heavy processing later with interrupts enabled. Traps and exceptions (page fault, divide-by-zero, the syscall instruction itself) are the synchronous cousins of interrupts.

DMA: with programmed IO the CPU copies every word between device and memory. A DMA engine instead is handed a buffer address and length, moves the data itself across the bus, and raises a single interrupt on completion - the CPU computes in the meantime. This is how disk and NIC transfers really happen. Zero-copy techniques (sendfile, mmap) attack the remaining kernel-to-user copies, not the DMA itself.

Process memory layout, low addresses to high: text (code, read+execute), rodata, data (initialized globals), bss (zero-initialized globals), heap growing upward via brk/sbrk, a large mmap region (shared libraries, thread stacks, file mappings, large allocations), and the main stack growing downward from the top, with ASLR randomizing the bases. malloc is a user-space allocator layered on top: small requests carve chunks from heap arenas; large ones (glibc threshold around 128KB) get a private mmap so that free() actually returns memory to the OS. Stack overflow hits a guard page and dies as SIGSEGV.

mmap vs read: mmap maps page-cache pages straight into your address space - no per-access syscall, no copy into a user buffer; pages fault in on first touch, and MAP_SHARED writes reach the file while MAP_PRIVATE gives copy-on-write. It shines for large files with random access and for sharing memory between processes; plain read/write with a decent buffer often wins for cold sequential streaming, where readahead works and you avoid per-page fault overhead and TLB churn.

qa.q: What lives in the PCB, and why does a context switch cost far more than the register save/restore suggests?
qa.a: The PCB (task_struct on Linux) holds the PID, process state, saved registers, kernel stack pointer, scheduling data (priority, vruntime), a pointer to the memory descriptor and page tables, the open-file table, signal state, and parent/child links. The direct switch - save registers, swap kernel stacks, restore - is well under a microsecond. The dominant cost is indirect: switching address spaces reloads CR3, which invalidates TLB entries unless they are tagged with ASIDs/PCIDs, and the incoming task starts on cold L1/L2 caches and branch predictors, running slowly for a while. That is why switching between threads of one process is cheaper (no address-space change) and why schedulers fight to preserve cache affinity.
must_hit: registers, state, page-table pointer, fds in PCB

qa.q: Process vs thread: what is shared, what is private, and when do you pick processes over threads?
qa.a: Threads of one process share the address space - code, globals, heap - plus the file descriptor table and signal dispositions. Private to each thread: registers (PC, SP), its stack, TLS, errno, and its scheduling state. Threads buy cheap creation and switching and zero-copy communication through shared memory, but zero isolation: one thread corrupting memory or segfaulting takes the whole process down. Processes are the unit of isolation - separate address spaces, independent failure, enforceable security boundaries - which is why Chrome runs sites in separate processes and why critical services prefer multi-process architectures. Rule: threads for tightly coupled parallel work on shared data; processes when you need fault isolation, privilege separation, or independent lifecycle.

qa.q: Explain fork/exec/wait. Why does Unix use two calls instead of a single spawn?
qa.a: fork() clones the caller - copy-on-write address space, copied fd table (sharing open-file offsets), same PC - and returns twice: child PID to the parent, 0 to the child. exec() replaces the current image with a new program, keeping the PID and non-CLOEXEC fds; on success it never returns. wait()/waitpid() blocks until a child exits and collects its status, reaping the zombie. The two-call split is deliberate: the code between fork and exec runs in the child and can rearrange its world - dup2 for redirection, wiring pipe ends, dropping privileges, changing directories - before the new program starts. A monolithic spawn would need a parameter for every such tweak; posix_spawn exists and is exactly that kind of parameter list.
must_hit: exec replaces image, never returns on success

qa.q: What is a zombie process, why does the OS create them at all, and how do you deal with them? What is an orphan?
qa.a: When a child exits, its exit status must survive until the parent asks for it, so the kernel keeps a PCB stub - PID plus status - in state Z. That stub is the zombie: it holds no memory or CPU, but it occupies a PID, and a forking server that never reaps will exhaust the PID space. You cannot kill it; it is already dead. Remedies: the parent calls wait/waitpid (typically a SIGCHLD handler looping waitpid with WNOHANG), sets SIGCHLD to SIG_IGN so the kernel auto-reaps, or uses the double-fork trick. An orphan is the reverse: the parent dies first, and the child is reparented to init or the nearest subreaper, which waits on it - so orphans get reaped automatically.

qa.q: User-level threads vs kernel-level threads: tradeoffs, and what happens when an M:1 user thread makes a blocking syscall?
qa.a: Kernel threads (1:1) are individually visible to the scheduler: they run in true parallel across cores and one can block in a syscall while siblings run, but create/switch operations cross into the kernel. User-level threads (M:1) are multiplexed by a library on one kernel thread: switching is a cheap register swap in user space and you can have millions, but the kernel sees a single thread - so one blocking syscall blocks every user thread in the process, and there is no multi-core parallelism. M:N runtimes (Go) schedule many green threads over a few kernel threads, intercepting blocking operations (netpoller, syscall handoff) to keep the kernel threads busy. Linux pthreads and std::thread are 1:1.
must_hit: blocking syscall blocks all M:1 threads

qa.q: fork copies nothing at first. Explain exactly how copy-on-write fork works, down to the page-table bits.
qa.a: fork duplicates the page tables, not the pages: parent and child PTEs point at the same physical frames, both marked read-only, with each frame's reference count incremented. Reads proceed freely. When either side writes, the MMU raises a protection fault; the kernel sees the VMA is logically writable, so instead of SIGSEGV it allocates a new frame, copies that single page, points the writer's PTE at the copy with write permission restored, decrements the shared frame's refcount, and resumes the instruction. If the refcount was already 1, it just flips the PTE writable. Cost of fork becomes O(page-table size), not O(memory); the common fork-then-exec pattern touches almost nothing before exec discards it all. vfork/posix_spawn skip even the table copy.
must_hit: fork cost = page tables, not memory

qa.q: Why is creating a thread cheaper than creating a process, and what does std::thread map to on Linux?
qa.a: Process creation must set up a new address space: duplicate page tables, copy the fd table, signal state, and accounting. Thread creation skips all of that - it allocates a stack and a small control block and registers a new schedulable entity that shares the existing mm, files, and signal handlers. On Linux both go through the same clone() syscall; the flags decide the sharing: CLONE_VM, CLONE_FILES, CLONE_SIGHAND, CLONE_THREAD for a thread, none of those for a fork-like process. So a Linux thread is literally a task that shares more. std::thread wraps pthread_create, a 1:1 kernel thread; remember that destroying a joinable std::thread calls std::terminate, so join or detach - or use C++20 std::jthread, which joins in its destructor.
must_hit: no new address space for threads

qa.q: Round robin: what goes wrong if the time quantum is too small, and if it is too large? How would you pick it?
qa.a: Too large: tasks usually finish or block before preemption, so RR degenerates into FCFS - response time for short interactive jobs collapses behind long bursts. Too small: the fixed cost of each context switch (microseconds of direct cost plus TLB and cache damage) is paid so often that a large fraction of CPU time goes to switching instead of work. You want the quantum to dwarf the switch cost - thousands of times larger - while staying small enough that interactive response holds; the classic heuristic is choosing q so roughly 80 percent of CPU bursts complete within one quantum. In practice that lands at a few milliseconds. Also quotable: with n runnable tasks, RR bounds any task's wait for its next slice at (n-1)q.

qa.q: Why is SJF optimal, why is it unimplementable as stated, and how do real systems get close?
qa.a: SJF minimizes average waiting time provably: in any schedule with a longer job before a shorter one, swapping them reduces the shorter job's wait more than it increases the longer one's - repeat until sorted (exchange argument). It is unimplementable because the next CPU burst length is unknown at decision time. Approximations: predict bursts with an exponential moving average, tau(n+1) = a*t(n) + (1-a)*tau(n), leaning on the fact that burst behavior is locally stable; or learn it structurally with MLFQ, where jobs that keep exhausting quanta sink to low priority and short/interactive jobs stay high - SJF behavior without measurement. SRTF, the preemptive variant, is optimal among preemptive policies but aggravates starvation of long jobs, so production systems pair any SJF-like policy with aging.
must_hit: MLFQ as the online approximation; starvation risk

qa.q: What is the convoy effect and which scheduling policy suffers from it? How is it fixed?
qa.a: Under non-preemptive FCFS, one process with a long CPU burst at the head of the queue forces every short, IO-bound process to wait behind it. While they wait, the IO devices they would have kept busy sit idle; when the CPU hog finally blocks, all the short jobs execute quickly, issue their IO, and the CPU goes idle - the system oscillates instead of overlapping CPU and IO, so both utilization and average waiting time suffer badly. The numeric classic: bursts 24,3,3 arriving in that order give 17ms average wait; reversed, 3ms. Fixes: preemption with a quantum (RR) so short jobs get through, or shortest-job-style ordering. The same word describes lock convoys, where threads queue behind a heavily contended mutex.
must_hit: short/IO-bound jobs stuck behind long burst
fix: RR preemption or SJF ordering

qa.q: Explain priority inversion with the Mars Pathfinder incident and the standard fixes.
qa.a: Priority inversion: a low-priority task holds a lock that a high-priority task needs; the high task blocks. If a medium-priority task now preempts the low one, the high-priority task is effectively waiting on the medium task - priority order is inverted, potentially unboundedly. On Mars Pathfinder (1997), a low-priority meteorological task held a shared bus mutex, medium-priority tasks kept preempting it, the high-priority bus management task missed its deadline, and the watchdog kept resetting the spacecraft. Fixes: priority inheritance - a lock holder temporarily inherits the highest priority among its waiters, so it cannot be preempted by middle tasks (this was enabled remotely on Pathfinder); or priority ceiling - each lock carries the priority of its highest possible locker, applied on acquisition. Also mention plain starvation of low priorities, cured by aging.
must_hit: low holds lock, high blocks, medium preempts

qa.q: How does MLFQ approximate SJF without knowing burst lengths, and how does it handle gaming and starvation?
qa.a: MLFQ keeps several ready queues at descending priority. New tasks enter the top queue; a task that consumes its full quantum is demoted a level, while one that blocks early stays high. Since interactive and IO-bound jobs have short bursts, they remain at high priority and get scheduled first, while CPU hogs sink - the system infers burst length from observed behavior, which is precisely what SJF needed to know in advance. Gaming: a task could yield just before quantum expiry to dodge demotion; the fix is charging total CPU time consumed at each level, demoting once the allotment is used regardless of how it was sliced. Starvation of demoted jobs: a periodic priority boost lifts everything back to the top queue, which also re-evaluates tasks whose behavior changed from batch to interactive.
must_hit: demote on quantum exhaustion, stay high if blocking early
infers burst behavior online = SJF approximation

qa.q: Give the Linux CFS elevator pitch: how it decides who runs, and how nice values enter.
qa.a: CFS models an ideal fluid processor where n tasks each get 1/n of the CPU. Each runnable task accumulates vruntime - real runtime scaled by a weight from its nice value (lower nice = heavier weight = vruntime grows slower = more CPU) - and the scheduler always runs the task with the smallest vruntime, held in a red-black tree keyed by vruntime for O(log n) pick and reinsert. There is no fixed quantum: a target latency window (a few ms, scaled by core count) is split among runnables with a minimum granularity floor. Newly woken sleepers get vruntime set near the current minimum - scheduled promptly, but no banked credit from sleeping. Closer: since kernel 6.6, CFS is replaced by EEVDF, which adds per-task latency deadlines on top of the same fairness accounting.
must_hit: smallest vruntime runs, red-black tree O(log n)

qa.q: What three properties must any critical-section solution satisfy, and what do real locks build on?
qa.a: Mutual exclusion: at most one thread inside at a time. Progress: if the section is empty, the choice of who enters next cannot be postponed indefinitely, and only threads actually trying to enter participate in the decision. Bounded waiting: once a thread requests entry, there is a bound on how many times others may enter ahead of it - no starvation by the protocol itself. Pure software solutions (Peterson's algorithm, two threads) satisfy all three on paper but assume sequentially consistent memory, which modern CPUs and compilers break without fences. Real locks are built on atomic read-modify-write hardware instructions - test-and-set, compare-and-swap, fetch-and-add - plus memory barriers. Disabling interrupts works only inside a uniprocessor kernel: other cores are unaffected and user code cannot do it.

qa.q: A mutex and a binary semaphore look identical. What is the real difference and why does it matter?
qa.a: Ownership. A mutex has an owner: the thread that locked it must be the one to unlock it. A binary semaphore has no owner: any thread may signal it, including one that never waited. That makes the semaphore a signaling primitive - thread A can complete a handoff by signaling a semaphore that thread B is waiting on, which is illegal with a mutex. Ownership is not pedantry: it is what lets the implementation do priority inheritance (boost the known holder), detect recursive locking and unlock-by-non-owner errors, and support robust mutexes that recover when an owner dies. Use a mutex to protect data; use semaphores to count resources or signal events. Using a binary semaphore as a lock forfeits inheritance and invites wrong-thread-unlock bugs.
must_hit: mutex has ownership, semaphore does not
ownership enables priority inheritance + error detection
mutex for data, semaphore for counting/events

qa.q: Spinlock vs sleeping mutex: when is each correct, and why do kernels ever spin?
qa.a: A spinlock busy-waits on an atomic flag: no context switch, but the waiting core does no useful work. It wins when the expected hold time is shorter than the cost of blocking and rescheduling - roughly a microsecond or two - and only on multiprocessors, since on a single core spinning prevents the very holder you are waiting for from running. It is mandatory in contexts that cannot sleep: interrupt handlers and kernel code holding a spinlock already. A sleeping mutex pays the switch but frees the core, correct for long or highly contended sections. Production locks are adaptive: futex-based mutexes spin briefly then sleep, uncontended acquisition never entering the kernel. Kernel discipline: while holding a spinlock, never sleep, and disable preemption (and possibly IRQs) on that core.
must_hit: spin when hold time < switch cost, SMP only
mutex sleeps: right for long sections
adaptive/futex hybrid; no sleeping under a spinlock

qa.q: Why must condition-variable waits always sit in a while loop, and why must wait release the mutex atomically?
qa.a: Two reasons for the loop. POSIX and C++ permit spurious wakeups - wait can return with no signal at all. More fundamentally, real systems use Mesa semantics: signal only moves a waiter to the ready queue; between being woken and reacquiring the mutex, another thread can run and consume the condition (e.g., steal the queue item), so the predicate must be rechecked: while (!pred) cv.wait(lk). The atomic release-and-sleep matters because of the lost wakeup race: if a thread released the mutex and then, before sleeping, the signaler ran - checked the state, signaled - the sleeper would sleep forever on a signal that already fired. C++ encodes the loop in cv.wait(lock, pred); notify_one when one waiter can proceed, notify_all when the predicate change affects all or waiters wait on different predicates.
must_hit: Mesa semantics: signal is a hint, predicate may be re-falsified

qa.q: Solve bounded-buffer producer-consumer with semaphores, and show the acquisition-order bug.
qa.a: Three primitives: counting semaphores empty initialized to N (free slots) and full to 0 (filled slots), plus a mutex for the buffer structure. Producer: wait(empty); lock(mutex); insert; unlock(mutex); signal(full). Consumer: wait(full); lock(mutex); remove; unlock(mutex); signal(empty). The counting semaphores handle blocking on full/empty states; the mutex handles the index updates. The bug: if the producer takes the mutex before wait(empty), then with a full buffer it sleeps on empty while holding the mutex - and the consumer, who alone can signal empty, blocks on that mutex. Deadlock. Rule: never sleep on a condition while holding the lock the condition's producer needs. With condvars: lock; while (count==N) wait(not_full); insert; signal(not_empty); unlock - the wait releases the mutex internally.
must_hit: wait on counting semaphore BEFORE the mutex
swapped order deadlocks when buffer full
condvar version: while loop, wait releases lock

qa.q: State the four Coffman conditions. Which one does global lock ordering break, and why does that actually work?
qa.a: Deadlock requires all four simultaneously: mutual exclusion (resources not shareable), hold-and-wait (a thread holds one resource while waiting for another), no preemption (resources released only voluntarily), and circular wait (a cycle of threads each waiting for the next one's resource). Global lock ordering breaks circular wait: number all locks; every thread acquires in ascending order. In any wait cycle, some thread would have to be holding a higher-numbered lock while waiting for a lower one - impossible under the discipline - so no cycle can form. It works because it needs no runtime machinery, just a convention (order by address is common). C++ gives it built-in: std::lock and std::scoped_lock acquire multiple mutexes with a deadlock-avoidance algorithm. Alternatives - trylock and release-all on failure - break hold-and-wait but can livelock.

qa.q: How does Banker's algorithm avoid deadlock, and what exactly is a safe state? Why is it rarely used?
qa.a: Banker's is deadlock avoidance: every process declares its maximum resource claim up front. When a request arrives, the algorithm pretends to grant it and asks: is the resulting state safe? A state is safe if there exists some ordering of all processes such that each can be run to completion using currently available resources plus those released by predecessors in the order - simulated by repeatedly finding a process whose remaining need fits in the available pool. If safe, grant; if not, block the requester even though resources are physically free. Key distinction: unsafe does not mean deadlocked - it means the system can no longer guarantee avoidance. It is rarely deployed because real processes cannot declare maximum claims, process counts fluctuate, and the O(m*n^2) safety check per request is unattractive; general-purpose OSes prefer the ostrich approach, databases prefer detection plus abort.
must_hit: impractical: unknown maxima; OSes use ostrich, DBs detect+abort

qa.q: Distinguish deadlock, livelock, and starvation, with one concrete example and one fix each.
qa.a: Deadlock: threads blocked forever, no state change - T1 holds A wanting B, T2 holds B wanting A. Fix: break a Coffman condition, canonically lock ordering. Livelock: threads keep executing and changing state but make no progress - both grab their first lock, trylock the second, fail, release everything, retry in perfect lockstep, forever; or two people sidestepping in a hallway. Fix: randomized backoff to break symmetry. Starvation: the system as a whole progresses, but a particular thread never gets its resource - writers never acquiring a reader-preference rwlock under continuous readers, or a low-priority task under strict priority scheduling. Fix: fairness - FIFO queuing of lock requests, aging that raises effective priority with waiting time. Framing that lands: deadlock is starvation of everyone; livelock is deadlock wearing running shoes.
must_hit: livelock: state changes, no progress; randomized backoff
starvation: others progress, one never does; aging/fairness

qa.q: What problems does virtual memory actually solve? Give the full list, not just isolation.
qa.a: Six wins. Protection and isolation: each process gets a private address space; a wild pointer cannot touch another process or the kernel. Overcommit: programs can exceed physical RAM via demand paging; only the working set needs residency. Uniform layout: every process sees the same address-space shape, simplifying linking, loading, and enabling ASLR. Sharing: one physical copy of libc's code maps into everyone; fork shares via COW. No external fragmentation of physical memory: any frame satisfies any page, so RAM never develops unusable holes. Memory-mapped files and IPC: file contents and shared segments appear as plain memory. Costs to acknowledge: MMU hardware and page tables, TLB pressure, page-fault handling, and a performance cliff (thrashing) when overcommit exceeds locality.
must_hit: no external fragmentation; mmap; costs acknowledged

qa.q: Why are page tables multi-level, and how does x86-64 translate a 48-bit virtual address?
qa.a: A flat table for a 48-bit space with 4KB pages needs 2^36 entries times 8 bytes - 512GB per process - almost all of it describing unmapped space. Multi-level tables turn the mapping into a sparse radix tree: x86-64 splits the 48 bits into four 9-bit indexes plus a 12-bit page offset. CR3 points at the PML4; each level's index selects an entry pointing at the next-level table (PML4 -> PDPT -> PD -> PT), and the leaf PTE yields the physical frame plus permission bits. Subtrees for unmapped regions simply do not exist, so sparse address spaces cost almost nothing. The price: a translation is now up to four dependent memory reads - which is why the TLB is essential and why 2MB/1GB huge pages (terminating the walk a level early) matter for big-memory workloads. Five-level paging extends to 57 bits.
must_hit: sparse tree: only mapped paths exist

qa.q: What is the TLB, what does a miss cost, and what happens to it on a context switch?
qa.a: The TLB is a small, highly associative cache inside the MMU mapping recent virtual page numbers to physical frames plus permissions - typically tens to a few thousand entries. A hit adds essentially nothing to the access; a miss on x86 triggers a hardware page-table walk of up to four memory reads before the original access proceeds. Effective access time = h*t + (1-h)*(t + walk). On a context switch that changes address spaces, untagged TLB entries would be stale and must be flushed - a major hidden switch cost. ASIDs/PCIDs fix this by tagging each entry with an address-space id so entries from multiple processes coexist. TLB reach = entries x page size; when a workload's hot set exceeds reach, huge pages multiply coverage 512-fold per entry, a standard database/HFT tuning lever.
must_hit: flush on switch unless ASID/PCID tagged
reach = entries x page size; huge pages

qa.q: Walk through page-fault handling, and distinguish minor from major faults.
qa.a: The MMU finds the PTE not-present (or a permission violation) and traps; the CPU saves state and enters the kernel's fault handler with the faulting address in a register (CR2). The kernel looks the address up in the process's VMA list. Not covered by any VMA, or permissions genuinely violated: deliver SIGSEGV. Covered: a minor fault requires no disk IO - the page is already in the page cache (shared library, previously read file), it is fresh anonymous memory (map the zero page or a new frame), or it is a COW break - fix the PTE and return. A major fault requires IO: allocate a frame, submit a disk read, block the thread while others run, and on the completion interrupt update the PTE and mark it present. Either way, the faulting instruction re-executes - hardware guarantees precise restart - and now succeeds.
must_hit: trap with faulting address, VMA validity check
minor: no IO (cache/zero/COW); major: disk read, thread blocks

qa.q: Compare FIFO, LRU, clock, and optimal replacement. What is Belady's anomaly and which algorithms are immune?
qa.a: Optimal evicts the page whose next use is farthest in the future - unimplementable online, but the yardstick every study compares against. LRU evicts the least recently used page, betting on temporal locality; near-optimal in practice but exact LRU needs per-reference timestamp or list maintenance, too expensive in hardware. Clock (second chance) is the practical approximation: frames in a circle, a hand sweeps clearing hardware-set reference bits, evicting the first page whose bit is already clear - referenced pages get a second lap. FIFO evicts the oldest page regardless of use, so a hot page loaded early gets evicted repeatedly; worse, FIFO exhibits Belady's anomaly: adding frames can increase faults (string 1,2,3,4,1,2,5,1,2,3,4,5 gives 9 faults with 3 frames, 10 with 4). LRU and OPT are stack algorithms - the pages held with k frames are always a subset of those held with k+1 - so they are provably immune.
must_hit: LRU good but exact form too costly
clock = reference-bit second chance, the practical choice
Belady: FIFO yes, stack algorithms (LRU/OPT) immune

qa.q: What is thrashing, how do you detect it, and how do the working-set model and page-fault frequency fix it?
qa.a: Thrashing is the collapse when the combined working sets of runnable processes exceed physical RAM: every process faults almost immediately on each quantum, the CPU sits idle waiting on the paging device, and a utilization-driven scheduler responds by admitting more processes - a positive feedback loop that makes it worse. Detection signature: CPU utilization low, paging IO saturated. The working-set model formalizes the cure: W(t, delta) is the set of pages a process referenced in its last delta references, approximating its current locality. Admit a process only if its working set fits in free frames; if the sum of working sets exceeds RAM, suspend entire processes (medium-term scheduling) rather than shaving frames off everyone. Page-fault frequency is the simpler feedback controller: measure each process's fault rate; above an upper threshold, grant it frames; below a lower one, reclaim frames; if no frames remain to grant, swap someone out.
must_hit: sum of working sets > RAM, feedback loop
working set W(t,delta), suspend whole processes
PFF thresholds as the control loop

qa.q: Internal vs external fragmentation: which does paging have, which does segmentation have, and why did paging win?
qa.a: Internal fragmentation is waste inside an allocated unit: you asked for 10KB, got three 4KB pages, and 2KB of the last page is dead - unusable by anyone else because it is allocated. External fragmentation is waste between allocations: total free memory is plentiful but scattered in holes too small to satisfy a request. Paging has only internal fragmentation (average half a page per region; proportionally worse with 2MB huge pages) because every frame is identical - any frame satisfies any page, so holes cannot form. Segmentation has only external fragmentation because segments are variable-length; after churn, physical memory becomes Swiss cheese and needs compaction, which means copying live data. Paging won because internal waste is small and bounded while compaction is expensive and unpredictable; the useful part of segmentation - named regions with permissions - survives as VMAs layered above paging.
must_hit: internal = waste inside allocation; external = scattered holes
paging: internal only, any frame fits
VMAs preserve the segment idea over paging

qa.q: Beyond fork, where is copy-on-write used, and what exactly happens on the write fault?
qa.a: Mechanism: physical frames shared between mappings are refcounted and every sharer's PTE is write-protected. A store hits the read-only PTE, the MMU raises a protection fault, and the kernel consults the VMA: the region is logically writable, so this is a COW break, not a SIGSEGV. If the frame's refcount exceeds one, allocate a new frame, copy the page, retarget the writer's PTE with write permission, decrement the old refcount; if the refcount is exactly one, just set the PTE writable in place. The instruction restarts. Users beyond fork: MAP_PRIVATE file mappings (your writes never reach the file), shared library data segments (one copy until a process writes), Linux KSM deduplicating identical pages across VMs, and snapshot semantics in general - Redis persistence forks precisely to get a COW frozen snapshot while the parent keeps serving writes.
must_hit: fault handler distinguishes COW from SIGSEGV via VMA
copy only if refcount > 1, else flip writable
uses: MAP_PRIVATE, lib data, KSM, snapshot-by-fork

qa.q: What does an inode store, what does it deliberately not store, and how does a path like /usr/bin/ls become data blocks?
qa.a: An inode stores a file's metadata: type, permission bits, owner uid/gid, size, timestamps, link count, and the map from file offsets to disk blocks - classically 12 direct pointers plus single, double, and triple indirect blocks; ext4 uses extents (start + length runs) instead. It does not store the filename: names live exclusively in directory entries, which are just (name, inode number) pairs inside directory files - that separation is what makes hard links possible and cheap. Resolution of /usr/bin/ls: start at the root inode (known number, typically 2), read its data blocks, find the entry usr and its inode number, load that inode, repeat for bin and ls, checking execute/search permission on each directory along the way. The kernel's dentry cache short-circuits repeat lookups; open() ends with an fd pointing through an open-file description to the in-memory inode.

qa.q: Hard links vs symbolic links: differences at the inode level, and when does deleted file data actually get freed?
qa.a: A hard link is a second directory entry referencing the same inode number; the inode's link count increments and both names are fully equal - there is no original. Hard links cannot cross filesystems (inode numbers are per-filesystem) and cannot target directories (would create cycles and break the tree). A symlink is a distinct inode of type link whose data is a pathname string, resolved afresh on every traversal: it can cross filesystems, name directories, and dangle if the target vanishes, at the cost of an extra resolution step. Freeing: rm calls unlink, which removes one name and decrements the link count. The inode and data blocks are reclaimed only when the link count reaches zero AND no process holds the file open - so a deleted-but-open log file keeps consuming disk (df full, du innocent), and deliberately unlinked open temp files self-destruct on close.
must_hit: hard link = extra dentry, same inode, count++
symlink = separate inode containing a path; can dangle/cross FS
no cross-FS or directory hard links
freed at link count 0 AND no open fds

qa.q: Trace a system call from user space into the kernel. Why is it a mode switch and not a context switch, and why do syscalls still cost real money?
qa.a: The libc wrapper loads the syscall number and arguments into registers per the ABI and executes the syscall instruction. The CPU raises the privilege level to ring 0, jumps to the fixed kernel entry point, and switches to the thread's kernel stack; the kernel saves user registers, dispatches through the syscall table, and validates every user-supplied pointer with copy_from_user/copy_to_user - the kernel never dereferences user addresses raw. sysret restores user mode with the return value in a register. It is a mode switch because the same task and address space continue - nothing is scheduled, no PCB swap. Costs remain: entry/exit overhead (tens to hundreds of ns, inflated by mitigations like KPTI), pipeline and cache disturbance. Hence the batching culture: stdio buffering, readv/writev, epoll, io_uring submitting many ops per crossing, and the vDSO answering clock_gettime with no kernel entry at all.
must_hit: registers + syscall instruction, ring 0, kernel stack

qa.q: Interrupts vs polling, and why DMA exists. What are top and bottom halves?
qa.a: Polling burns CPU checking device status in a loop - fine when responses arrive within nanoseconds (some HFT NIC paths poll deliberately), wasteful otherwise. Interrupts invert control: the device raises an IRQ, the CPU finishes its current instruction, saves minimal state, and vectors through the interrupt table to a handler - the CPU does useful work until the device actually needs it. Without DMA, the CPU still must copy every word between device and memory (programmed IO). A DMA engine takes a buffer address and length, transfers the block itself over the bus, and raises one interrupt at completion - CPU and IO genuinely overlap. Linux splits handlers because interrupt context cannot sleep and blocks other IRQs: the top half acknowledges the hardware and queues work in minimal time; the bottom half (softirq, tasklet, workqueue) does protocol processing later with interrupts enabled. NAPI even switches the NIC from interrupts back to polling under load to prevent interrupt livelock.
must_hit: DMA: device-memory transfer without CPU, one completion interrupt
top half minimal/ack, bottom half heavy work

qa.q: Lay out a process's address space, explain where malloc actually gets memory, and compare mmap vs read for file IO.
qa.a: Low to high: text (code, read+execute), rodata, data (initialized globals), bss (zero-filled globals), heap growing up via brk, a large mmap region holding shared libraries, thread stacks, and file mappings, then the main stack growing down from near the top; ASLR randomizes the bases and a guard page turns stack overflow into SIGSEGV. malloc is a user-space allocator: small requests are carved from arena chunks obtained via brk/sbrk (freeing returns them to the arena, rarely to the OS); large requests (glibc threshold about 128KB) get a dedicated mmap, so free() genuinely unmaps. File IO: read() is a syscall per call plus a copy from page cache to your buffer; mmap maps the page-cache pages into your space - no copy, no per-access syscall, pages faulting in on touch. mmap wins for large files, random access, and sharing; buffered read often wins for cold sequential streaming via readahead, without per-page fault and TLB costs.
must_hit: text/data/bss/heap up, mmap region, stack down, ASLR + guard page
small malloc from brk arenas; large via mmap (returned on free)
read = syscall + copy; mmap = page-cache mapping, zero copy
mmap for random/large/shared; read for sequential streaming

units.title: Transactions & ACID
units.cold_probe: Schedule: r1(A), w2(A), r2(B), w1(B). Decide whether it is conflict-serializable (show the test), and explain what would have happened to this interleaving under two-phase locking.
units.brief:
A transaction is a unit of work with four guarantees. Atomicity: all-or-nothing, implemented via undo information in the write-ahead log — on abort or crash, uncommitted changes are rolled back. Consistency: each transaction moves the database between valid states; the engine enforces declared constraints (PK, FK, CHECK), but application invariants are the application's contract — and ACID-C is not CAP-C (the latter means linearizability across replicas). Isolation: concurrent execution behaves as if serial, enforced by locking or MVCC, tunable via isolation levels. Durability: once commit is acknowledged the redo log has been fsynced; data pages flush lazily.

Schedules and serializability. Two operations conflict iff they belong to different transactions, touch the same item, and at least one is a write (RW, WR, WW). A schedule is conflict-serializable iff its precedence graph — edge Ti -> Tj when an operation of Ti conflicts with and precedes one of Tj — is acyclic; any topological order gives an equivalent serial schedule. This on-paper test is the single most examined skill in the unit. View-serializability is broader (admits blind-write schedules) but NP-complete to test, so engines target conflict-serializability.

Two-phase locking: a growing phase (acquire S locks to read, X locks to write) followed by a shrinking phase (after the first release, acquire nothing more). Theorem: 2PL admits only conflict-serializable schedules. But plain 2PL prevents neither deadlock nor cascading aborts (T2 reading T1's uncommitted write must abort if T1 aborts). Strict 2PL holds X locks until commit/abort (rigorous: all locks), making schedules cascadeless and recoverable — this is what real engines run.

Deadlocks. Detection: maintain a waits-for graph, find cycles, abort a victim (InnoDB picks the transaction with the least undo work) and let the application retry. Prevention by timestamp: wait-die (older waits, younger requester dies) and wound-wait (older aborts the younger holder, younger waits) — each allows waiting in only one age direction, so no cycles, and aborted transactions keep their timestamp, so no starvation. Timeouts are the blunt fallback. Application discipline: lock rows in a consistent global order and keep transactions short — the same rule as ordered std::mutex acquisition in C++.

Isolation levels map onto the anomalies they eliminate:
- Read Uncommitted: dirty reads possible (reading data that may be rolled back).
- Read Committed: no dirty reads; non-repeatable reads remain (a row re-read mid-transaction has changed).
- Repeatable Read: row re-reads are stable; the standard still permits phantoms (a re-run predicate query sees new matching rows). InnoDB's RR blocks most phantoms with next-key (record+gap) locks; Postgres RR is snapshot isolation.
- Serializable: equivalent to some serial order — the only level that also kills write skew.

MVCC. Keep multiple versions of each row stamped with creating/deleting transaction IDs; a reader sees the newest version committed before its snapshot, so readers never block writers and writers never block readers (writer-writer conflicts still lock). Read Committed snapshots per statement; Repeatable Read per transaction. Old versions live in undo logs (InnoDB) or the heap until VACUUM (Postgres). Snapshot isolation prevents dirty, non-repeatable, and phantom reads but permits write skew: two transactions each read an overlapping invariant and write disjoint rows — both doctors verify 'someone is on call' and both go off call. Postgres Serializable (SSI) detects the dangerous rw-antidependency pattern and aborts one.

Optimistic vs pessimistic concurrency control. Pessimistic locks before access — constant overhead, wins under high contention. Optimistic runs read -> validate -> write on a private snapshot and aborts on conflict — wins when conflicts are rare; it is the mutex vs CAS-retry-loop tradeoff from C++.

Durability mechanics worth naming: WAL rules — the log record reaches disk before the modified page (undo rule) and before commit is acknowledged (redo rule); group commit batches fsyncs; checkpoints bound recovery; ARIES recovers by analysis, redo-all-history, then undo of losers with compensation log records.
must_hit: Conflict pairs: r1(A)-w2(A) gives edge T1 -> T2; r2(B)-w1(B) gives edge T2 -> T1
Precedence graph has a cycle, so the schedule is NOT conflict-serializable
Acyclic precedence graph is the exact test; topological order = equivalent serial schedule

Under 2PL this interleaving cannot occur — T1 could not release A before acquiring B, so T2 simply blocks until T1 commits and execution degenerates to the serial order T1 -> T2; no deadlock arises, because T2 requests A before B and therefore never holds B while waiting on A
2PL guarantees conflict-serializability but not deadlock-freedom; strict 2PL additionally prevents cascading aborts
misconceptions: ACID consistency is the same as CAP consistency - it is not; ACID-C means application invariants hold across a transaction, CAP-C means linearizable reads across replicas
2PL prevents deadlocks - it only guarantees serializability; deadlocks need separate detection (waits-for graph) or prevention (wait-die/wound-wait)
Repeatable Read always allows phantoms - the standard permits them, but InnoDB RR blocks most via next-key locks and Postgres RR (snapshot isolation) has none; the SI cost is write skew instead
MVCC means no locks at all - readers skip locks, but writers still lock rows against other writers; MVCC removes reader-writer blocking only
rapid: Conflicting ops: same item, different transactions, at least one write
Precedence graph acyclic <=> conflict-serializable
Strict 2PL = hold X locks to commit => cascadeless + recoverable
Dirty read = read uncommitted; non-repeatable = row changed on re-read; phantom = new rows match a re-run predicate
Snapshot isolation's signature anomaly: write skew (overlapping reads, disjoint writes)
WAL: log before data page (undo), log before commit-ack (redo)
Wait-die: older waits, younger dies; wound-wait: older wounds, younger waits
Optimistic CC = read, validate, write; wins at low contention, thrashes at high

units.title: Indexing & storage
units.cold_probe: Why is virtually every database index a B+ tree rather than a balanced BST or a hash table — and name two situations where adding an index makes the system slower.
units.brief:
Database cost is measured in page I/Os, not comparisons — the core mental shift from competitive programming. Tables live in fixed-size pages (8 KB in Postgres, 16 KB in InnoDB) inside heap files; the buffer pool caches pages in RAM; a predicate with no usable index means reading every page of the table (sequential scan). Everything about index design falls out of minimizing page reads.

B+ tree: internal nodes hold only routing keys; all records or row pointers live in the leaves; leaves are linked left-to-right. A node fills a page, so fanout is in the hundreds, and height is log_fanout(N): 3-4 levels index a billion rows, with the root and internal levels essentially always hot in the buffer pool — a point lookup costs about one real I/O. Why not a balanced BST? One key per node means log2(N) ~ 30 pointer chases, each a potential random page read, with terrible locality. Why not a hash index? O(1) point lookups but no range predicates, no ORDER BY, no prefix matching (LIKE 'abc%') — while the B+ tree's linked leaves make range scans sequential and nearly free. B+ trees also stay balanced under churn (page splits/merges) and have mature concurrent implementations (latch crabbing).

Clustered vs secondary. A clustered index stores the table itself in key order — the leaf level IS the data — so there is exactly one per table; InnoDB always clusters on the primary key. A secondary index maps key -> row locator; in InnoDB the locator is the PK value, so a secondary lookup does two tree descents: secondary tree to PK, PK tree to row. Two consequences interviewers love: a fat PK (UUID, wide composite) bloats every secondary index, and random-order PK inserts (UUIDv4) cause constant page splits, which is why compact auto-increment surrogates are the default advice. Postgres tables are heaps: all indexes point at heap tuple IDs (ctid); CLUSTER reorders once but is not maintained.

Covering and composite indexes. A covering index contains every column the query needs, so the engine answers with an index-only scan and never touches the heap/PK tree — often the single biggest practical optimization. Composite indexes obey the leftmost-prefix rule. An index on (a, b, c) serves:
- a = ?
- a = ? AND b = ? (and adding c = ?)
- a range alone, or a = ? with a range on b
Once a range condition appears on a column, columns to its right cannot be used for seeking (only filtered). It does nothing for predicates on b or c alone. Design rule: equality columns first, then the range or ORDER BY column; matching ORDER BY to index order removes a sort node entirely.

When indexes hurt:
- Write amplification: every INSERT/DELETE, and every UPDATE of an indexed column, must maintain every index; bulk loads drop and rebuild indexes for exactly this reason.
- Low selectivity: if a predicate matches a large fraction of rows, one random heap hop per row loses to a sequential scan around single-digit-percent selectivity — the optimizer, using per-column statistics (histograms, distinct-value counts), will rightly ignore the index, and you keep paying its write cost.
- Function-wrapped columns: WHERE YEAR(created_at) = 2026 defeats an index on created_at — rewrite as a half-open range or create a functional index.
- Leading wildcards: LIKE '%xyz' cannot seek a B+ tree.

Verification habit: EXPLAIN (ANALYZE) is the source of truth for which index actually ran; stale statistics flip plans, which is why production incidents often end with ANALYZE. In HLD crossovers, know the one-line contrast: B+ trees update pages in place (read-optimized), LSM trees batch writes into sorted runs with background compaction (write-optimized — RocksDB, Cassandra).
must_hit: The cost model is disk pages: fanout of hundreds gives height 3-4 for billions of rows, vs ~30 random I/Os for a BST
Linked leaves make range scans, ORDER BY, and prefix LIKE sequential — a hash index does O(1) point lookups but none of these
Slower case 1: write-heavy tables — every index adds maintenance to each INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE
Slower case 2: low-selectivity predicates — per-row random heap hops lose to a sequential scan, and the optimizer will ignore the index (statistics-driven)
misconceptions: An index on (a,b) helps queries filtering only on b - it does not; the leftmost-prefix rule means the index is sorted by a first
More indexes always make queries faster - each index taxes every write, and on low-selectivity columns the optimizer will not even use it
Clustered index just means 'the index on the primary key' - clustered means the table rows are physically stored inside the index; Postgres PK indexes are non-clustered heap pointers
B+ tree lookups cost log2(N) steps - fanout is in the hundreds, so real trees are 3-4 levels deep and the upper levels are cached
rapid: Page = unit of I/O: 8 KB Postgres, 16 KB InnoDB
Height ~ log_fanout(N): fanout in the hundreds => 3-4 levels for 10^9 rows
One clustered index per table; InnoDB secondary lookup = secondary tree, then PK tree
Covering index => index-only scan, no heap visit
Composite rule: equality columns first, range column last usable
LIKE 'abc%' can use a B+ tree; LIKE '%abc' cannot
WHERE f(col) = x defeats a plain index on col — rewrite or use a functional index
Random UUID PKs cause page splits and bloat every InnoDB secondary index

units.title: Design & normalization
units.cold_probe: Enroll(student, course, instructor) with FDs {student,course} -> instructor and instructor -> course. Find the candidate keys, the highest normal form this relation satisfies, and state exactly what you lose if you decompose it to BCNF.
units.brief:
Keys, precisely: a superkey is any attribute set that functionally determines all attributes; a candidate key is a minimal superkey (a relation may have several); the primary key is the chosen candidate key; a prime attribute belongs to at least one candidate key — the 2NF/3NF definitions hinge on this word; a foreign key references another relation's candidate key and enforces referential integrity. Natural keys carry meaning but change and can be wide; surrogate keys (auto-increment) are stable and compact — doubly important in InnoDB, where the PK rides along in every secondary index.

Functional dependencies: X -> Y means any two tuples agreeing on X agree on Y, in every legal instance — a semantic rule, not a fact about current data. Armstrong's axioms (reflexivity, augmentation, transitivity) generate all implied FDs, but the working tool is the attribute closure X+: repeatedly add the right side of any FD whose left side is inside the set. X is a superkey iff X+ = all attributes; F implies X -> Y iff Y is inside X+. Candidate-key hunting: attributes appearing on no right-hand side must be in every key; grow from there with closures.

The normal forms, each killing one redundancy class:
- 1NF: atomic values, no repeating groups or arrays-in-a-cell.
- 2NF: no non-prime attribute depends on a proper subset of a candidate key (only biting when keys are composite).
- 3NF: no transitive dependencies — for every nontrivial X -> A, X is a superkey OR A is prime.
- BCNF: for every nontrivial X -> A, X is a superkey. No escape clause.

Worked example. Enroll(student, course, instructor), FDs: {student, course} -> instructor; instructor -> course. Closures give two candidate keys: {student, course} and {student, instructor} — so course and instructor are both prime. The FD instructor -> course has a non-superkey determinant, so BCNF fails; but course is prime, so 3NF holds. The relation is in 3NF, not BCNF, and redundancy remains: each instructor's course repeats once per student — an update anomaly waiting to happen. The BCNF decomposition, (instructor, course) + (student, instructor), is lossless, but the FD {student, course} -> instructor now spans two tables and cannot be enforced by any single-table constraint: dependency preservation is lost. The theorem to quote: 3NF always admits a lossless, dependency-preserving decomposition (the synthesis algorithm); BCNF guarantees losslessness only.

Why normalize at all — the anomalies. Update anomaly: one fact in many rows, so a change must hit them all or the database contradicts itself. Insertion anomaly: cannot record a fact without an unrelated one (no department row until it has an employee). Deletion anomaly: deleting the last carrier of a fact destroys the fact. Decomposition puts each fact in exactly one place.

Denormalization is a deliberate, managed reversal: when read cost dominates — hot multi-join queries at high QPS, reporting workloads, precomputed aggregates — you re-introduce redundancy on purpose. Star schemas in analytics (one wide fact table plus dimension tables) are denormalized by design; document databases embed instead of joining. The professional answer always includes the consistency plan: triggers, transactional dual-writes, materialized views, or async rebuild jobs.

ER-to-relational mapping, the mechanical rules:
- Strong entity -> table keyed by its own key; weak entity -> table keyed by owner key + partial key.
- 1:N relationship -> foreign key on the N side; 1:1 -> FK on either side (prefer the total-participation side).
- M:N relationship -> junction table whose composite PK is both FKs, carrying any relationship attributes (Enrollment(student_id, course_id, grade)).
- Multivalued attribute -> its own table keyed by (owner key, value).
must_hit: instructor -> course violates BCNF (instructor is not a superkey) but passes 3NF because course is prime
BCNF decomposition (instructor,course) + (student,instructor) is lossless but loses enforcement of {student,course} -> instructor — dependency preservation is sacrificed
3NF always allows lossless + dependency-preserving decomposition; BCNF does not
misconceptions: Higher normal forms are always better - normalization trades write anomalies for join cost; OLAP star schemas denormalize deliberately
2NF bans every dependency on a non-key column - 2NF only bans partial dependencies on part of a composite candidate key; with a single-attribute key, 1NF implies 2NF
You can always reach BCNF without losing anything - the Enroll example proves dependency preservation can be unachievable in BCNF; only 3NF guarantees both
Any unique column can be the primary key with no consequences - natural keys change and wide keys bloat InnoDB secondary indexes; prefer compact surrogates
rapid: FD X -> Y: agree on X => agree on Y, in every legal instance
