Net guarantee: O(n log n) worst case (required by the standard since C++11). std::sort is NOT stable - equal elements may reorder; std::stable_sort is a merge sort, O(n log n) with a temporary buffer and O(n log^2 n) if allocation fails. std::sort requires random-access iterators (so not std::list - use list::sort); std::nth_element does top-k selection in O(n) average via introselect.
must_hit: quicksort + depth-limit heapsort fallback + insertion finish
O(n log n) worst case guaranteed

qa.q: What is small string optimization (SSO) and what are its consequences?
qa.a: std::string reserves a small buffer INSIDE the string object itself and stores short strings there - typically up to 15 characters in libstdc++ and MSVC, 22 in libc++ - so short strings need zero heap allocation and enjoy perfect locality. The pointer/size/capacity fields and the inline buffer share the object's footprint via a union-like layout. Consequences worth naming: creating and copying short strings is allocation-free and fast; MOVING a short string cannot steal a heap buffer, so it copies the bytes - string move is therefore not always O(1)-pointer-swap, and pointers or iterators into a small string do not survive its move; capacity() starts at the SSO size, not zero; and SSO is exactly why std::string is a distinct beast from vector<char>, which never stores elements inline. This is a classic 'do you know your ABI' depth question.
must_hit: short strings stored inline, no heap (15/22 chars)
moves of small strings copy bytes; pointers do not survive

qa.q: What formally constitutes a data race, and what are the fix strategies?
qa.a: The C++ memory model definition: two or more threads access the same memory location, at least one access is a write, and the accesses are not ordered by a happens-before relationship (no common mutex, no atomic synchronization, no thread join). Any execution containing a data race has UNDEFINED BEHAVIOR - not merely stale reads: the compiler optimizes assuming races are impossible, so racy code can be miscompiled arbitrarily. Canonical example: counter++ from two threads is load-add-store; interleavings lose increments. Fixes, in order of preference: eliminate sharing (per-thread accumulation, then a single reduce after join - usually fastest); make the variable std::atomic and use fetch_add (lock-free, per-operation cost); or guard the data with a mutex via lock_guard (simplest, blocking). Verify with ThreadSanitizer, which detects the happens-before violations at runtime.
must_hit: same location, >=1 write, no happens-before => UB
fixes: no sharing > atomic > mutex; TSan

qa.q: Compare lock_guard, unique_lock, and scoped_lock.
qa.a: All three are RAII wrappers over mutexes - the shared point is that manual lock()/unlock() is banned because early returns and exceptions skip the unlock. lock_guard: minimal and zero-overhead; locks in its constructor, unlocks in its destructor, offers nothing else - the default for simple critical sections. unique_lock: a flexible, movable handle - supports std::defer_lock, try_lock, timed locking, and manual unlock()/lock() cycles mid-scope; it is REQUIRED by condition_variable::wait, which must release and reacquire the mutex around sleeping; costs a flag of state over lock_guard. scoped_lock (C++17): variadic - locks MULTIPLE mutexes in one shot using the std::lock deadlock-avoidance algorithm, which acquires them in a globally consistent way; the fix for AB/BA lock-ordering deadlocks (the bank-transfer example). Rule: scoped_lock or lock_guard by default; unique_lock only when a condition variable or mid-scope unlocking demands it.

qa.q: Why must condition_variable::wait take a predicate (or be wrapped in a loop)?
qa.a: Two failure modes make a bare wait() wrong. Spurious wakeups: the implementation may return from wait without any notify - permitted explicitly by the standard because of how futex-style primitives behave - so waking is not proof the condition holds. Lost wakeup: if the producer sets the condition and notifies BEFORE the consumer calls wait, the notification is gone and a bare wait sleeps forever. The predicate form, cv.wait(lk, pred), is defined as while (!pred()) wait(lk): it checks the condition under the mutex before first sleeping (curing lost wakeups) and rechecks after every wake (curing spurious ones). Correct protocol: the producer modifies the shared state while holding the SAME mutex, then calls notify_one or notify_all; the consumer's predicate reads that state under the lock. State changed outside the mutex can still race.
must_hit: lost wakeup if condition set before wait
wait(lk,pred) == while(!pred()) wait; state mutated under same mutex

qa.q: List five common sources of UB and explain why UB enables optimization.
qa.a: Common UB: (1) signed integer overflow - unsigned wraps, signed does not; (2) out-of-bounds array/vector indexing; (3) dereferencing null or dangling pointers (use-after-free, returning a reference to a local); (4) reading uninitialized variables; (5) data races; also invalid shifts (negative or >= bit width) and strict-aliasing violations. The optimization argument: the standard lets the compiler assume UB never occurs, and every such assumption is an optimization license. Because signed overflow is UB, x+1 > x folds to true and int-indexed loops vectorize without a wraparound case. Because null dereference is UB, a null check appearing after a dereference is provably dead and gets deleted. Because races are UB, values stay cached in registers across statements. That is why UB does not mean crash - it can mean tests pass and production misbehaves. Development safety net: -fsanitize=undefined,address.
must_hit: compiler assumes UB never happens - the license for optimization
x+1>x folds true / null-check deletion example; sanitizers

qa.q: What is the One Definition Rule and what is a common way to violate it?
qa.a: ODR: within a single translation unit, no entity may be defined twice; across the whole program, every non-inline function or variable that is odr-used must have EXACTLY one definition. Classes, templates, and inline functions may be defined in multiple TUs - which is how headers work - but the definitions must be token-for-token identical with the same meaning. The teeth: violations are ill-formed with NO DIAGNOSTIC REQUIRED - the linker may silently choose one definition and drop the rest. Classic violations: a non-inline, non-template function defined in a header included by two TUs (duplicate-symbol error if lucky); two different classes sharing a name in different TUs of one program; the same header compiled under different macros (NDEBUG altering a member) so one class has two layouts - silent memory corruption. Mitigations: inline or static for header definitions, unnamed namespaces for TU-local types, consistent build flags.
must_hit: one definition program-wide for non-inline entities
no diagnostic required; header/macro mismatch example

units.title: SOLID & design principles (bridge to LLD)
units.cold_probe: A teammate adds a new shape type and has to edit a 200-line switch inside area(), plus three other files that switch on the same enum. Which SOLID principles are being violated, and how would you restructure this in C++ (give both a runtime and a compile-time mechanism)?
units.brief:
SOLID is the working vocabulary of low-level-design rounds. Interviewers rarely ask you to recite the acronym; they watch whether the classes you sketch for "design a parking lot / Splitwise / rate limiter" quietly obey it, then probe with "what if requirements change to X?" Every principle is a defense against a specific change-related pain. Learn each letter as: definition, C++ mechanism, failure it prevents.

- S, Single Responsibility: a class has one reason to change - one actor or axis of requirements - not "one method". A TradeReport that computes P&L, renders HTML, and writes files couples finance, presentation, and persistence: a formatting change risks pricing code, and the math cannot be unit-tested without a filesystem. Split into PnlCalculator, ReportFormatter, ReportWriter, composed by a thin orchestrator.
- O, Open/Closed: add behavior by extension, not by modifying tested code. The smell it cures is switch-on-type duplicated across the codebase, each copy needing an edit per new type. C++ gives two mechanisms: virtual dispatch through an abstract base (runtime), and templates/concepts or CRTP (compile time, zero vtable cost - say this explicitly in HFT rooms).
- L, Liskov Substitution: a derived object must work anywhere the base is expected without breaking caller assumptions. Square-from-Rectangle: setWidth on a Square silently changes height, breaking code written against Rectangle's contract. Contract rules: a derived class may not strengthen preconditions, weaken postconditions, or break base invariants. If you are tempted to violate them, use composition instead of inheritance.
- I, Interface Segregation: no client should be forced to depend on methods it does not use. Split fat interfaces into small role interfaces - in C++, small pure-abstract classes (IReader, IWriter) rather than one IStorage with twelve pure virtuals of which each client needs two. Fat interfaces also force mocks to implement irrelevant methods.
- D, Dependency Inversion: high-level policy depends on abstractions; low-level detail implements them. Constructor-inject an IOrderSender& (or unique_ptr) into OrderEngine; production wires the FIX gateway, tests inject a mock. Templating the engine on the sender type is the compile-time version of the same inversion.

The supporting trio. DRY: every piece of knowledge has one authoritative representation - it is about knowledge, not textually similar lines; merging two coincidentally identical fragments that serve different business rules creates the "wrong abstraction", which costs more than duplication. Rule of three: tolerate the second copy, abstract on the third. KISS: choose the simplest design that meets today's requirements; every layer of indirection must be paid for by a real, present need. YAGNI: do not build speculative generality - in an interview, say "I would introduce a Strategy interface here only when a second algorithm actually appears", which signals judgment, not ignorance of patterns.

Coupling vs cohesion is the deeper goal all of the above serves. Cohesion measures how strongly the elements inside a module belong to one purpose; coupling measures how much modules know about each other's internals. Target: high cohesion within, low coupling between. Symptoms to name: shotgun surgery (one logical change touches five files) means coupling is too high; classes you can only name with "And" or "Manager" mean cohesion is too low. C++-specific point that lands well: #include fan-out and rebuild times are a measurable form of coupling - forward declarations, interface headers, and pimpl reduce compile-time coupling, not just conceptual coupling.

Bridge to the LLD round: clarify requirements, list entities, define interfaces before implementations, and narrate trade-offs in this vocabulary. When the interviewer changes a requirement, your design should absorb it by adding a class, not editing ten. Present patterns (Strategy, Factory, Observer) as instances of OCP/DIP rather than name-drops - "Strategy is just DIP applied to an algorithm choice" is a hire-signal sentence.
must_hit: SRP = one reason to change (axis of change, not one method)
OCP via abstract base + virtual OR templates/CRTP; kills duplicated switch-on-type
LSP contract: no stronger preconditions, no weaker postconditions/invariants
DIP = inject abstractions (testability); ISP = small role interfaces
High cohesion / low coupling is the umbrella goal
DRY is about knowledge; wrong abstraction costs more than duplication
misconceptions: SRP means one class = one method or 'does one thing' - correction: it means one reason to change; std::vector has dozens of methods and one responsibility
Open/Closed means you never modify existing code - correction: bug fixes modify code; OCP means new variation arrives through extension points, not edits to stable code
Inheritance is the primary code-reuse tool - correction: inheritance is for substitutability (LSP is-a); prefer composition for reuse
More patterns = better design - correction: KISS/YAGNI; speculative patterns add indirection and coupling with no payoff
rapid: SRP: one reason to change, per actor/axis of requirements
OCP in C++: virtual dispatch (runtime) or templates/concepts/CRTP (compile time, zero vtable cost)
LSP litmus: derived may not strengthen preconditions or weaken postconditions
ISP: split fat interfaces into role interfaces (small pure-abstract classes)
DIP: depend on abstract base or template param; enables mock injection in tests
High cohesion within modules, low coupling between them - SOLID's real goal
YAGNI: no speculative generality; rule of three before abstracting duplication
Composition over inheritance by default; inherit only for substitutability

units.title: Linux & shell essentials
units.cold_probe: A service on a Linux box is pinning a CPU core at 100%. Give the exact commands to find the process, inspect what it is doing (files, memory, syscalls), and stop it gracefully - and state precisely what you escalate to if it ignores you, and why that escalation cannot be caught.
units.brief:
Linux questions test whether you have operated software, not just written it. The bar: given a misbehaving box, can you find the process, see what it is doing, and act - and do you know what each command actually does to the kernel.

Processes. Every process has a PID and a parent PID; new processes come from fork() (clone the caller) followed by exec() (replace the image). ps aux lists everything; top/htop sort live by CPU or memory; pgrep name finds PIDs by process name (safer than ps | grep, which matches its own grep line). Job control: cmd & runs in background, Ctrl-Z suspends (SIGTSTP), jobs/fg/bg manage them; nohup or tmux keeps work alive after the SSH session dies, because a closing terminal sends SIGHUP.

Signals are the control plane. kill PID sends SIGTERM (15) - catchable, so a well-written service flushes buffers and exits cleanly. SIGKILL (9) and SIGSTOP are never delivered to the process; the kernel enforces them, so no cleanup runs - which is why the correct escalation is TERM, wait, then KILL. SIGINT is Ctrl-C; SIGSEGV is the kernel's response to an invalid memory access. A zombie is a child that exited but whose parent has not called wait(): it holds only a process-table entry, consumes no CPU or RAM, and cannot be killed - it is already dead; reap it via the parent (or kill the parent so init/systemd adopts and reaps it). An orphan is the opposite: a live child whose parent died - harmless, adopted by init.

The text-processing pipeline is the everyday superpower. grep -rn "pat" dir searches recursively with line numbers (-i case-insensitive, -v invert, -E extended regex, -l filenames only, -c count). find dir -name '*.log' -type f -mtime -1 -size +100M locates files by predicate; act on results with -exec cmd {} \; or pipe to xargs. Pipes connect one command's stdout to the next's stdin; the canonical frequency counter is sort | uniq -c | sort -nr | head (uniq needs sorted input). tail -f follows a growing log; wc -l counts lines; awk '{print $2}' and cut -d: -f1 extract columns; sed 's/old/new/g' substitutes.

Redirection: > truncates, >> appends, 2> redirects stderr, and 2>&1 means "point fd2 at whatever fd1 points to right now" - so order matters: cmd > out 2>&1 sends both to the file, while cmd 2>&1 > out leaves stderr on the terminal. Pipes carry only stdout, so add 2>&1 before | grep if you want errors too.

Permissions: three rwx triplets - owner, group, other; octal r=4 w=2 x=1, so chmod 640 = rw-r-----. Directory semantics differ: r lists names, w creates/deletes/renames entries, x traverses (cd and reach inodes). Deleting a file requires write on its directory, not on the file. chmod u+x adds selectively; chown changes ownership (root only to give away).

ssh user@host for remote shells; key auth via ssh-keygen and the remote ~/.ssh/authorized_keys; scp/rsync copy files; ssh -L 8080:localhost:80 host forwards a port - enough for any "how do you work on a remote box" probe.

/proc mental model: a virtual filesystem - files generated by the kernel at read time, zero disk. It is the raw source that ps, top, and lsof merely reformat. Per process: /proc/PID/status (state, VmRSS, threads), /proc/PID/fd/ (every open descriptor - count them to spot FD leaks), maps (memory mappings and loaded .so files), cmdline, exe, cwd. System-wide: /proc/meminfo, /proc/cpuinfo, /proc/loadavg. Knowing this lets you debug inside a minimal container with no tools installed.

Canonical scenario answer, verbatim-ready: top to find the PID -> ls /proc/PID/fd and cat /proc/PID/status (or strace -p PID for syscalls) to see what it is doing -> kill PID and wait -> kill -9 PID only if TERM is ignored, accepting that no cleanup will run.
must_hit: kill = SIGTERM (catchable, cleanup) vs kill -9 = SIGKILL (kernel-enforced, uncatchable); escalate TERM then KILL
Zombie = exited child awaiting parent wait(); kill cannot remove it
2>&1 order matters; pipes carry stdout only
Directory x bit = traverse; delete needs w on the directory, not the file
grep/find/xargs + sort|uniq -c|sort -nr as the core pipeline
misconceptions: kill -9 is the normal way to stop a process - correction: SIGKILL skips all handlers so no cleanup runs; send SIGTERM first and escalate only if ignored
A zombie is consuming resources and must be killed - correction: it is already dead, holding only a PID-table entry until the parent wait()s; signal or fix the parent instead
Files under /proc occupy disk - correction: /proc is a virtual filesystem whose contents the kernel synthesizes on every read
chmod 777 is a fix for permission errors - correction: it hands full control to everyone; find the missing bit (often directory x) and grant minimally
rapid: kill sends SIGTERM(15) by default; SIGKILL(9) and SIGSTOP cannot be caught or ignored
pgrep/pkill match process names; ps aux | grep matches its own grep line
2>&1 duplicates stderr onto stdout's current target - order relative to > matters
chmod 754 = rwxr-xr--; octal r4 w2 x1; x on a directory = traverse
Zombie: dead, unreaped, unkillable; orphan: alive, adopted by init
sort | uniq -c | sort -nr = frequency table; uniq requires sorted input
nohup/tmux survive SSH disconnect (SIGHUP kills plain jobs)
/proc/PID/fd counts open descriptors - the quick FD-leak check

units.title: Git working knowledge
units.cold_probe: Explain the difference between git reset --hard HEAD~1 and git revert HEAD: what each does to the branch pointer, index, working tree, and history; which one is safe on a branch your teammates share and why; and how you would recover if you ran the wrong one.
units.brief:
Git questions separate people who memorized commands from people who hold the object model. Hold the model and every command becomes predictable.

The model. A commit is a content-addressed object: a tree (a full snapshot of every file, stored as hashed blobs - not a diff), zero or more parent commit hashes (a root/initial commit has none; a merge has several), author/committer metadata, and a message; its own hash covers all of that, which makes history tamper-evident and means "editing" a commit actually creates a new one. Storage stays cheap because unchanged files reuse the same blob and packfiles delta-compress internally - but the mental model is snapshots; diffs are computed on demand. A branch is nothing but a 41-byte file containing a commit hash - a movable pointer that advances when you commit. HEAD points at the current branch; pointing HEAD at a raw commit is detached HEAD. Merge commits have two or more parents (an octopus merge has more than two); history is a DAG.

Merge vs rebase. git merge feature creates a merge commit with both histories as parents: nothing is rewritten, the true parallel structure is preserved, the graph gets noisy. git rebase main replays your commits one by one onto main, minting new commits with new hashes: linear, clean history - but it is rewriting. Golden rule: never rebase commits others may have built on (anything pushed to a shared branch); their clones now disagree with yours and pulls produce duplicated commits. Etiquette that answers 90% of git questions: rebase your private feature branch freely, merge (or squash-merge) into shared branches.

Undo, correctly chosen. All git reset variants move the branch pointer to a target commit; the flag decides what else follows: --soft moves only the pointer (abandoned changes sit staged), --mixed (default) also resets the index (changes become unstaged edits), --hard also resets the working tree (uncommitted work is gone for good). Because reset rewrites the branch's visible history, it belongs only on commits you have not pushed. git revert HEAD instead creates a new commit applying the inverse patch - history moves forward, nothing rewritten - so it is the correct undo on any shared branch. Safety net: git reflog records every position HEAD has held (defaults: 90 days for reachable entries via gc.reflogExpire, 30 days for unreachable ones via gc.reflogExpireUnreachable); a "lost" commit after a bad reset or rebase is recovered with git reflog then git reset --hard <hash>. Of the uncommitted work --hard destroys, anything that was staged (git add) still exists as dangling blobs recoverable via git fsck --lost-found; only never-staged working-tree edits are truly unrecoverable.

Daily tools. git stash shelves dirty work (stash push -m msg, stash pop, stash list; -u includes untracked files) - the standard move before an urgent branch switch. git cherry-pick <hash> copies one commit onto the current branch. git bisect binary-searches history for the commit that introduced a bug: bisect start, bisect bad, bisect good <ref>, then answer good/bad as git checks out midpoints - about 10 tests for 1000 commits; automate with git bisect run ./test.sh (exit 0 good, nonzero bad, 125 skip). It is the highest-leverage git tool almost nobody uses; mentioning it unprompted is a signal.

Conflicts. A conflict means both sides changed the same lines. Git stops and marks the file: <<<<<<< HEAD (yours), =======, >>>>>>> branch (theirs); set merge.conflictStyle=diff3 to also see the common ancestor - it shows what each side changed from, which is usually the fastest route to the right resolution. Edit to the correct final content (often a blend, not a side), delete the markers, build and test, git add the file, then git merge --continue or git rebase --continue (--abort bails out). Trap worth naming: during rebase, ours/theirs are swapped relative to merge, because "ours" is the base being replayed onto.

Clean PR workflow: branch from fresh main (git switch -c feat/x), commit in small logical units with imperative messages that say why, rebase onto origin/main before opening the PR so the diff is honest, push and let CI go green, address review in new commits, keep current via rebase + git push --force-with-lease (refuses to clobber others' pushes, unlike bare --force), squash-merge, delete the branch.
must_hit: Commit = content-addressed snapshot + parent pointers; branch = movable pointer; HEAD = current position
Merge preserves history; rebase rewrites it; never rebase shared/pushed commits
reset --soft/--mixed/--hard scope (pointer/index/working tree); revert = safe inverse commit on shared branches
reflog recovers committed work after bad reset/rebase
bisect = O(log n) binary search for the bad commit; automatable with bisect run
PR hygiene: rebase private branch, force-with-lease, squash-merge
misconceptions: Commits are stored as diffs - correction: each commit references a full snapshot (tree of content-addressed blobs); diffs are computed on demand
Rebase is dangerous and merge is safe - correction: rebase is safe on private branches; the only danger is rewriting commits others already based work on

reset --hard permanently destroys the commits it abandons - correction: reflog keeps them (30 days by default once unreachable) and reset --hard <reflog-hash> restores; staged-but-uncommitted content survives as dangling blobs (git fsck --lost-found) - only never-staged edits are lost forever
revert removes a commit from history - correction: revert appends a new inverse commit; both commits remain visible
rapid: Branch = 41-byte file holding a commit hash; committing just moves the pointer
Golden rule: never rebase commits that have been pushed and shared
reset: --soft pointer only, --mixed +index (default), --hard +working tree
revert = forward-moving inverse commit; the shared-branch undo
git reflog = time machine for HEAD positions (defaults: 90 days reachable / 30 days unreachable)
bisect run ./test.sh automates the binary search; exit 125 = skip commit
During rebase, ours/theirs swap meaning versus merge
push --force-with-lease, never bare --force, on your own PR branch only

units.title: Testing & debugging discipline
units.cold_probe: A C++ service segfaults roughly once a day in production and never under the debugger. Lay out your complete plan: how you capture the crash, the tools you run and in what order (with build flags), and the loop you follow from first stack trace to verified fix with a regression test.
units.brief:
Interviewers probe testing and debugging to see whether you produce evidence or guesses. The hire signal is a systematic loop plus the right tool at each step.

Test taxonomy. A unit test exercises one function or class in isolation, collaborators replaced by test doubles: milliseconds fast, deterministic, and it pinpoints the failing unit. An integration test wires real components together - database, network, filesystem - and catches the wiring, config, and contract bugs unit tests cannot see, at the price of speed and flakiness. End-to-end tests drive the whole system through its public surface. The test pyramid - many unit, fewer integration, few e2e - exists because feedback speed and fault localization degrade as you climb. Good unit tests are FIRST: Fast, Independent (pass in any order), Repeatable (no hidden environment), Self-validating (binary pass/fail), Timely. Test behavior and contracts, not implementation details, or every refactor breaks green tests. Every bug you fix earns a regression test that fails before the fix and passes after. Coverage measures lines executed, not behaviors asserted - 100% coverage with empty assertions proves nothing.

Test doubles, precisely. Dummy: passed but never used. Stub: returns canned answers so the code under test can run; you assert on resulting state or output (state verification). Fake: a real but lightweight implementation, like an in-memory repository. Mock: records calls so the test asserts on the interactions themselves - called once, with these arguments (behavior verification). Spy: a stub that also records. Prefer state verification - it survives refactoring; reach for mocks when the interaction is the contract (must publish exactly one event). In C++ this is gMock: MOCK_METHOD plus EXPECT_CALL, made possible by injecting an abstract interface - Dependency Inversion is what makes code testable, which is the cleanest bridge between this unit and SOLID. Over-mocking couples tests to internals and makes suites brittle.

gdb, the working set. Build -g -O0 (symbols, no optimizer reordering). gdb ./app, run args. On a crash: bt for the stack, frame N to move, print var / p *ptr / info locals to inspect. break file.cpp:42, conditional b func if x==7, then next (step over) vs step (into), continue. watch var breaks when the memory changes - the tool for "who is corrupting this field?". Post-mortem: ulimit -c unlimited to enable cores, then gdb ./app core shows the dying stack; gdb -p PID attaches to a live process.

Memory tooling. Valgrind memcheck runs the unmodified binary on a synthetic CPU: no recompile, catches leaks, invalid reads/writes, use of uninitialized values - at 20-50x slowdown. AddressSanitizer instruments at compile time (-fsanitize=address -g): redzones and quarantine at ~2x slowdown, so it runs in CI and under realistic load, and it catches stack and global buffer overflows that valgrind cannot see. UBSan (-fsanitize=undefined) traps signed overflow, bad shifts, null misuse; TSan finds data races (run separately - incompatible with ASan); MSan covers uninitialized reads but needs clang and fully instrumented dependencies. Default recipe: ASan+UBSan on every CI test run; valgrind when you cannot rebuild.

The systematic loop. Reproduce: get a minimal, deterministic trigger and shrink it - everything downstream is iteration speed on this repro. Isolate: binary-search the failure space - which commit (git bisect), which half of the input, which layer - cut the space instead of staring at code. Hypothesize: one specific, falsifiable claim ("p is freed on the error path, then reused"). Verify: design the experiment that would disprove it - breakpoint, assertion, log - run it, then fix or loop with new data. Change one variable per experiment: change two and a pass tells you nothing, and you may have merely perturbed a race. Heisenbug that vanishes under the debugger? Suspect UB, data races, or uninitialized memory - go straight to sanitizers. After the fix: re-run the original repro, add the regression test, and grep for the same pattern elsewhere.

Logging is debugging at 3 AM without a debugger. Levels with intent: ERROR = failed and needs action, WARN = anomalous but handled, INFO = key lifecycle and business events, DEBUG/TRACE = development detail, runtime-switchable. Structure entries (JSON or key=value) and always carry a correlation/request ID so one request traces across services. Log at boundaries and decision points; never log secrets, tokens, or PII; keep logging off hot paths or make it async - synchronous formatting and I/O in a tight loop destroys latency.
must_hit: Pyramid: many unit / fewer integration / few e2e; FIRST properties; test behavior not implementation
Stub = canned state (state verification) vs mock = interaction assertions (behavior verification); DIP enables injection
gdb: -g -O0, break/run/next-vs-step/print/bt/watch; core dumps + gdb app core
ASan ~2x with recompile vs valgrind 20-50x without; UBSan/TSan/MSan roles
Loop: reproduce -> isolate -> hypothesize -> verify, one change per experiment
Every fixed bug gets a regression test; logging = levels + structure + correlation IDs, no secrets
misconceptions: Mocks and stubs are interchangeable words - correction: a stub feeds canned data for state-based assertions; a mock is itself the assertion, verifying interactions
Valgrind and ASan are equivalent, pick either - correction: valgrind needs no rebuild but runs 20-50x slower; ASan needs recompilation, runs ~2x, and catches stack/global overflows valgrind misses
100% coverage means well tested - correction: coverage counts executed lines, not asserted behaviors; assertion-free tests reach 100% while proving nothing
gdb next and step do the same thing - correction: next steps over a function call; step descends into it
rapid: Test pyramid: unit >> integration >> e2e; ice-cream cone = the anti-pattern
FIRST: Fast, Independent, Repeatable, Self-validating, Timely
Stub = state verification; mock = behavior verification; fake = working lightweight impl
gdb: b file:line, r, n over, s into, p var, bt, watch var; gdb -p PID attaches
Cores: ulimit -c unlimited, then gdb ./app core for the post-mortem stack
CI default: -fsanitize=address,undefined; TSan separately for races
Heisenbug or vanishes-under-debugger: suspect UB/races/uninitialized - run sanitizers
Change one variable per debugging experiment or the result is uninterpretable

qa.q: Explain Single Responsibility with a concrete C++ violation and its fix. What does SRP not mean?
qa.a: SRP: a class should have one reason to change - one actor or axis of requirements. Violation: a TradeReport class that computes P&L, formats HTML, and writes to disk mixes three change axes (finance logic, presentation, persistence). A formatting change risks touching pricing code, and the math cannot be unit-tested without a filesystem. Fix: split into PnlCalculator, ReportFormatter, and ReportWriter, composed by a thin orchestrator - each independently testable, each change localized. SRP does not mean one method per class: std::vector has dozens of methods but exactly one responsibility, owning a contiguous sequence. The test is whether two different stakeholders could independently demand changes to the same class.

qa.q: What does Open/Closed mean in practice, and what are the two C++ mechanisms for achieving it?
qa.a: OCP: new behavior arrives by adding code at designed extension points, not by editing existing tested code - open for extension, closed for modification. The smell it cures is switch-on-type logic duplicated across the codebase, where every new type means edits in many places. C++ mechanism one: runtime polymorphism - an abstract Shape base with virtual area(); adding Hexagon is one new class, and code holding Shape* never changes. Mechanism two: compile-time polymorphism - templates/concepts or CRTP; any type satisfying the concept plugs in with zero virtual-dispatch cost, which matters in latency-critical code. Caveat worth stating: OCP does not forbid modification (bug fixes edit code), and extension points should exist only where change is genuinely expected - YAGNI otherwise.

qa.q: Why does Square inheriting from Rectangle violate Liskov Substitution, even though a square is mathematically a rectangle? State the general contract rule.
qa.a: LSP demands that code written against the base class stays correct when handed any derived object. Rectangle's implied contract: setWidth leaves height unchanged. Square must override setters to keep sides equal, so client code doing r.setWidth(4); r.setHeight(5); assert(r.area()==20) breaks when r is a Square. Mathematically square is-a rectangle; behaviorally, as a mutable object with independent setters, it is not - substitutability is about behavior contracts, not taxonomy. General rule: a derived class may not strengthen preconditions (demand more of callers), weaken postconditions or class invariants (deliver less), or throw surprises base-class callers cannot expect. When tempted to violate this, drop the inheritance: use composition or make both immutable value types under a common interface.

qa.q: How do Interface Segregation and Dependency Inversion combine to make a C++ design testable? Sketch the shape of the code.
qa.a: ISP: no client depends on methods it does not use - split fat interfaces into role interfaces. DIP: high-level policy depends on abstractions, injected rather than constructed internally. Combined: an OrderEngine that only sends orders depends on struct IOrderSender { virtual void send(const Order&) = 0; virtual ~IOrderSender() = default; }; injected via constructor reference or unique_ptr. Production wires in FixGatewaySender; a unit test injects a mock that records calls - no network, deterministic, fast. ISP keeps the mock tiny (one method, not twelve); DIP is what makes injection possible at all - a class that news its own gateway is untestable. Compile-time variant: template the engine on the sender type - identical inversion, no vtable, standard in HFT code.
must_hit: mock in tests / real impl in prod

qa.q: Define coupling and cohesion, give the symptom of each going wrong, and name a C++-specific form of coupling.
qa.a: Cohesion: how strongly the elements inside a module belong to one focused purpose - high cohesion means the class does one job. Coupling: how much modules know about each other's internals - low coupling means few, narrow, abstract dependencies. Goal: high cohesion within, low coupling between; SOLID is largely machinery for this. Symptoms: shotgun surgery - one logical change forces edits in five files - means coupling is too high; a class you can only name with "And" or "Manager" (ReportGeneratorAndUploader) means cohesion is too low. C++-specific: #include fan-out is measurable compile-time coupling - touching one header rebuilds half the project. Depending on interface headers, forward declarations, and pimpl cuts it. Good module boundaries follow change: things that change together live together.
must_hit: cohesion = internal togetherness, coupling = inter-module knowledge

qa.q: DRY versus YAGNI: when is keeping duplication the better engineering decision?
qa.a: DRY targets knowledge, not text: every business rule should have one authoritative representation. But two fragments that look identical while serving different business rules will diverge; unifying them yields an abstraction that sprouts parameters and conditionals with each divergence - the "wrong abstraction", which costs more than the duplication it removed (Sandi Metz's argument). Rule of three: tolerate the second copy, abstract on the third occurrence, when the true invariant is visible. YAGNI complements this: do not pre-build general mechanisms for variation that may never arrive - speculative flexibility is coupling you pay for daily. Interview framing: duplication is cheap to fix later (extract when the shape is proven); a bad abstraction is expensive to unwind because callers grow around it. Optimize for reversibility.

qa.q: What exactly happens when you run kill on a process? Contrast SIGTERM, SIGKILL, and SIGSTOP, and give the correct escalation order.
qa.a: kill PID sends SIGTERM (15): a catchable request - a well-written service's handler flushes buffers, closes sockets, releases locks, and exits cleanly. SIGKILL (9) is never delivered to the process at all; the kernel destroys it immediately - no handlers, no cleanup, so you risk corrupt state, half-written files, and orphaned lock files. SIGSTOP pauses and SIGCONT resumes; SIGSTOP, like SIGKILL, cannot be caught, blocked, or ignored - kernel-enforced. Correct escalation: kill PID, give it seconds to shut down, kill -9 only if TERM is ignored. Also worth having ready: SIGINT is Ctrl-C, SIGSEGV is raised on invalid memory access, SIGHUP fires when the controlling terminal closes - the reason nohup and tmux exist.

qa.q: Explain what `ps aux | grep nginx | awk '{print $2}' | xargs kill` does, identify its bugs, and give the robust replacement.
qa.a: ps aux lists all processes; grep nginx keeps lines containing "nginx"; awk '{print $2}' extracts column 2, the PID; xargs kill passes those PIDs as arguments to kill, sending SIGTERM to each. Bugs: first, the grep process itself matches - its own command line contains "nginx" - so you may signal a PID that has already exited (classic fixes: grep '[n]ginx' or grep -v grep). Second, it over-matches any process whose arguments mention nginx, like vim nginx.conf - you would kill your editor. Robust replacement: pgrep nginx, which matches process names only, piped to xargs kill - or simply pkill nginx. Knowing pgrep/pkill and the self-match pitfall is the difference between having read about pipes and having operated systems.

qa.q: Decode chmod 640. What do r, w, and x mean on a directory, and what permission actually controls deleting a file?
qa.a: Permissions are three rwx triplets - owner, group, other - with octal r=4, w=2, x=1. 640 = rw-r-----: owner reads and writes, group reads, others get nothing. On a regular file x means executable. On a directory the semantics shift: r lets you list entry names (ls), w lets you create, delete, and rename entries, and x lets you traverse - cd into it and reach inodes by name. So x without r allows opening dir/known_name but not listing; r without x shows names you cannot access. Key trap: deleting a file is controlled by write permission on its directory, not by the file's own bits - a read-only file in a writable directory is deletable. chown changes ownership; only root may give files away.
must_hit: directory: r list, w create/delete, x traverse
deletion governed by directory w bit

qa.q: What is a zombie process, how does it arise, why can't you kill it, and how do you actually get rid of one? Contrast with an orphan.
qa.a: A zombie is a process that has exited but whose parent has not yet called wait()/waitpid() to collect its exit status. The kernel keeps only its process-table entry - PID and exit code - so the parent can read it; it consumes no CPU or memory and shows as Z or <defunct> in ps. kill is useless because signals target running code and the process is already dead. Remedies: make the parent reap - signal it (SIGCHLD nudges some), or fix its bug (missing SIGCHLD handling / wait loop); or kill the parent, whereupon the zombie is reparented to init/systemd, which reaps automatically. An orphan is the inverse: a live child whose parent died - harmless, adopted by init. Many zombies accumulating means a buggy parent, not a haunted box.
must_hit: only a PID-table entry, no resources
cannot be killed - reap via parent or kill parent
orphan = live child adopted by init

qa.q: What's the difference between `cmd > out 2>&1` and `cmd 2>&1 > out`? Why do pipes miss stderr by default?
qa.a: Redirections apply left to right, and 2>&1 means "point fd 2 at whatever fd 1 points to right now" - a duplication of the current target, not a live link. cmd > out 2>&1: stdout is first bound to the file, then stderr is duplicated onto it - both streams land in out. cmd 2>&1 > out: stderr is duplicated onto stdout's current target - the terminal - then stdout alone moves to the file, so errors stay on screen. A pipe connects only the left command's stdout to the right's stdin, so cmd | grep x never sees stderr; write cmd 2>&1 | grep x to include it. Related: >> appends, 2> redirects stderr alone, &> is bash shorthand for both, and tee writes to a file and the screen simultaneously.
must_hit: 2>&1 = duplicate onto current target of fd1

qa.q: What is /proc, and name the specific entries you'd read while debugging a misbehaving process on a box with no tooling installed.
qa.a: /proc is a virtual filesystem: its files occupy no disk and are synthesized by the kernel at read time, exposing kernel and per-process state. It is the raw source that ps, top, and lsof merely reformat - which is why knowing it lets you debug inside a minimal container. For a suspect PID: /proc/PID/status (run state, VmRSS memory, thread count, UIDs), /proc/PID/fd/ (every open descriptor - ls | wc -l is the instant FD-leak check), /proc/PID/maps (memory mappings and loaded .so files), /proc/PID/cmdline, exe, and cwd (exact invocation, binary, working directory), and /proc/PID/wchan or /proc/PID/stack (where it is blocked in the kernel - stack usually requires root to read). System-wide: /proc/meminfo, /proc/cpuinfo, /proc/loadavg. Most entries are plain text readable with cat, with exceptions worth knowing: exe and cwd are symlinks - read them with readlink or ls -l (cat /proc/PID/exe would dump the ELF binary itself) - and cmdline is NUL-separated, so cat runs the arguments together; pipe it through tr '\0' ' ' to see them spaced.
must_hit: status for state/memory, maps for mappings

qa.q: What is a git commit under the hood? Are commits diffs or snapshots, and what is a branch, really?
qa.a: A commit is a content-addressed object containing: a tree - a full snapshot of the project, with every file stored as a hashed blob - zero or more parent commit hashes (a root/initial commit has none), author/committer metadata, and the message. The commit's own hash covers all of that, making history tamper-evident and meaning any "edit" to a commit actually mints a new object. So commits are snapshots, not diffs; diffs are computed on demand by comparing trees. Storage stays cheap because unchanged files reuse identical blob hashes and packfiles delta-compress internally. A branch is nothing but a small file containing one commit hash - a movable pointer that advances on each commit. HEAD points to the current branch; pointing it at a raw commit is detached HEAD. Merge commits carry two or more parents (an octopus merge has more than two), making history a DAG.

qa.q: Merge versus rebase: what does each do to history, and what is the golden rule and its reason?
qa.a: git merge creates a merge commit with both branch tips as parents: no existing commit changes, the true parallel structure is preserved, and the graph gets noisy. git rebase replays your commits one by one onto the new base, minting new commits with new hashes: linear, reviewable history - but it is history rewriting. Golden rule: never rebase commits others may have based work on, i.e., anything pushed to a shared branch. Reason: their clones still hold the old hashes; after your rewrite, pulls resurrect the originals alongside the copies - duplicate commits and diverged history. Standard etiquette answering most git questions: rebase your private feature branch onto main to stay current (then push --force-with-lease to your own PR branch); merge or squash-merge into shared branches.

qa.q: Compare git reset --soft, --mixed, --hard, and git revert: what does each touch, and which is safe on a shared branch?
qa.a: Every reset moves the current branch pointer to the target commit; the flag decides what follows. --soft: pointer only - changes from abandoned commits remain staged. --mixed (default): pointer plus index - changes become unstaged working-tree edits. --hard: pointer, index, and working tree - uncommitted work is destroyed irrecoverably; committed work remains recoverable for ~30 days via git reflog then reset --hard <hash>. Because reset rewrites the branch's visible history, it belongs only on commits not yet pushed - on a shared branch it forces divergence for everyone. git revert creates a new commit applying the inverse patch of the target: history only moves forward, nothing is rewritten, so it is the safe and correct undo for anything already on a shared branch or main.
must_hit: hard kills uncommitted work; reflog rescues committed
revert = new inverse commit, shared-branch safe

qa.q: How does git bisect work, what's its complexity, and how do you fully automate it?
qa.a: bisect binary-searches history for the commit that introduced a regression. git bisect start; git bisect bad (current commit is broken); git bisect good v2.1 (a known-good ref). Git checks out the midpoint of the range; you test and answer git bisect good or bad; each answer halves the candidate set - about 10 tests locate the culprit among 1000 commits, O(log n). git bisect reset returns you to where you started. Full automation: git bisect run ./test.sh - git runs the script at every step; exit 0 marks good, 1-127 marks bad, and 125 means skip (commit untestable, e.g., does not build). Prerequisites: a deterministic reproducer and a history where most commits compile - one of the best arguments for keeping every commit green.
must_hit: bisect run with exit codes (125 = skip)

qa.q: Walk through resolving a merge conflict end to end, including the marker syntax and the ours/theirs trap under rebase.
qa.a: A conflict arises when merge or rebase finds the same lines changed on both sides; git stops and marks the files (git status shows "both modified"). Inside each file: <<<<<<< HEAD is your side, ======= divides, >>>>>>> branch is theirs. Setting merge.conflictStyle=diff3 adds the common-ancestor block - seeing what both sides changed from is usually the fastest path to the correct resolution. Edit the region to the true final content - often a manual blend of both, not a side-pick - delete all markers, build and run tests, then git add the file to mark it resolved and git merge --continue (or git rebase --continue; --abort bails out cleanly). Trap: during rebase, ours and theirs swap meaning relative to merge, because "ours" is the base branch you are replaying onto, not your feature work.

qa.q: Describe a clean PR workflow from branch creation to merge. Where do rebase and --force-with-lease fit, and why not plain --force?
qa.a: Branch from fresh main: git switch -c feat/x. Commit in small logical units with imperative messages explaining why, not what. Before opening the PR: git fetch and git rebase origin/main so the diff is against current main, run the tests, push, open the PR with intent and testing notes; CI green is a precondition. Address review feedback in new commits so reviewers see deltas. Keep the branch current by rebasing onto main again and pushing with --force-with-lease: it refuses to overwrite if anyone else pushed to the branch meanwhile, whereas bare --force silently destroys their work - that safety check is the entire point. Finish with squash-merge (one clean commit per PR) or rebase-merge per team convention, and delete the branch. Never rebase or force-push main itself.
must_hit: rebase onto main for honest diff

qa.q: Unit versus integration tests: define both, explain the test pyramid's reasoning, and list the FIRST properties.
qa.a: A unit test exercises one function or class in isolation, with collaborators replaced by test doubles: milliseconds fast, deterministic, and a failure pinpoints the unit. An integration test wires real components - database, network, filesystem - and catches the wiring, configuration, and contract mismatches unit tests structurally cannot see, at the cost of speed, flakiness, and coarse fault localization. The pyramid - many unit, fewer integration, few end-to-end - exists precisely because feedback speed and debuggability degrade as you climb; its inversion, the ice-cream cone of mostly manual/e2e testing, produces slow suites nobody runs. Good unit tests are FIRST: Fast, Independent (any order), Repeatable (no hidden environment), Self-validating (binary pass/fail), Timely. Test behavior and contracts, not implementation, and add a regression test for every bug fixed.

qa.q: Mocks versus stubs versus fakes - define each, explain state versus behavior verification, and say which to prefer and why.
qa.a: All are test doubles. Stub: returns canned answers (getPrice() always 100) so the code under test can execute; the test then asserts on resulting state or output - state verification. Mock: records the calls made to it, and the test asserts on those interactions - send() called exactly once with order X - behavior verification. Fake: a genuine but lightweight implementation, like an in-memory map standing in for a repository. Dummy: passed to satisfy a signature, never used. Prefer state verification: it survives refactoring, because tests do not encode which methods were called internally. Reach for mocks only when the interaction is the contract - must publish exactly one event, must never call twice. In C++: gMock's MOCK_METHOD and EXPECT_CALL, enabled by injecting an abstract interface - Dependency Inversion is what makes any of this possible. Over-mocking welds tests to implementation details.
must_hit: prefer state; mocks when interaction is the contract

qa.q: You have a reproducible segfault in a C++ binary. Walk through the gdb session, including build flags, the core commands, and the post-mortem path.
qa.a: Rebuild with symbols and no optimization: g++ -g -O0 (optimized builds reorder and inline, making stepping misleading). gdb ./app, then run args. On SIGSEGV gdb halts at the faulting instruction: bt prints the full stack, frame N jumps to a frame, print var / p *ptr / info locals inspect values - is the pointer null, garbage, or pointing into freed memory? Set break file.cpp:42 or a conditional b func if x==7, then next steps over calls, step descends into them, continue resumes. watch var breaks when the memory changes - the tool for "who corrupts this field?". Production crash: ulimit -c unlimited enables core dumps; gdb ./app core gives the post-mortem stack; gdb -p PID attaches live. If the stack is garbage or the crash wanders, the corruption happened earlier - switch to ASan.
must_hit: next vs step; watch for corruption
core dump post-mortem (ulimit, gdb app core)

qa.q: Valgrind versus AddressSanitizer: how does each work, what are the costs, and what does each catch that the other cannot? Name the other sanitizers and their roles.
qa.a: Valgrind memcheck runs your unmodified binary on a synthetic CPU, shadowing every memory access: no recompilation needed; catches leaks, invalid reads/writes, and use of uninitialized values; costs 20-50x slowdown - often too slow for realistic workloads or races that need timing. AddressSanitizer instruments at compile time (-fsanitize=address -g): redzones around allocations, quarantined frees, shadow memory; roughly 2x slowdown, so it runs in CI and under real load; it additionally catches stack and global buffer overflows, which valgrind cannot see without redzones. Uninitialized reads under ASan need MSan instead (clang, requires fully instrumented dependencies). UBSan (-fsanitize=undefined) traps signed overflow, invalid shifts, and null misuse; TSan detects data races and must run separately - incompatible with ASan. Practical policy: ASan+UBSan on every CI test run; valgrind when you cannot rebuild the binary.

qa.q: Describe the systematic debugging loop step by step, and justify the rule 'change one thing at a time'.
qa.a: Reproduce: obtain a minimal, deterministic trigger, and shrink both input and code until failure is seconds away - every later step iterates on this repro, so its speed is your debugging velocity. Isolate: binary-search the failure space rather than reading code - which commit (git bisect), which half of the input, which layer of the stack; each experiment should cut the space. Hypothesize: state one specific, falsifiable claim - "p is freed on the error path and reused" - not "something's wrong with memory". Verify: design the experiment that would disprove it (breakpoint, assertion, targeted log), run it, then fix or loop back with the new evidence. One variable per experiment: change two things and a pass tells you nothing about which mattered - and you may have merely perturbed timing on a race, hiding the bug rather than fixing it. Close the loop: re-run the original repro, add a regression test, and search for the same pattern elsewhere in the codebase.

qa.q: How do you log effectively in a production service - levels, structure, what to include, what to forbid, and the performance angle?
qa.a: Levels with intent: ERROR - failed and needs action; WARN - anomalous but handled; INFO - key lifecycle and business events (request served, order placed); DEBUG/TRACE - development detail, off in production but switchable at runtime without redeploy. Structure every entry (JSON or key=value) so machines can parse it, and always carry timestamp, level, component, and a correlation/request ID - the ID is what lets you trace one request across services; an uncorrelatable line is noise. Log at boundaries and decision points, not every statement. Forbidden: secrets, tokens, passwords, PII - both a security hole and a compliance violation. Performance: synchronous formatting and I/O inside a hot loop destroys latency - the HFT-relevant point - so use async or buffered sinks, sample high-volume events, and guard expensive DEBUG formatting behind level checks. Logs are the debugger that is always attached at 3 AM.
must_hit: async sinks / keep off hot paths

lld.title: Parking Lot
lld.worked:
Requirements. A multi-floor lot; vehicle types motorcycle, car, truck; spot types motorcycle, compact, large (handicapped and EV arrive later as extensions — design for that). Multiple entry and exit gates operate concurrently. Entry issues a ticket bound to an assigned spot; exit computes a fee from duration and vehicle type, takes payment, frees the spot. Allocation prefers the nearest available spot to the gate. Per-floor display boards show free counts by type. The functional list is easy; the real evaluation is whether allocation stays correct under concurrent gates and whether a pricing or allocation change touches one class or ten.

Entities and relationships. ParkingLot owns vector<Floor>. Floor owns its spots. ParkingSpot { SpotId id; int floor; SpotType type; std::atomic<SpotState> state; } — spots vary by data, not behavior, so SpotType is an enum, not a class hierarchy. Vehicle carries VehicleType plus plate; the vehicle-to-allowed-spot-types mapping is a table inside the allocator (motorcycle -> {motorcycle, compact, large}, car -> {compact, large}, truck -> {large}), so adding a vehicle type is a table row, not a class plus downcasts. Ticket { TicketId, plate, SpotId, entryTime } lives in a TicketRegistry (unordered_map keyed by TicketId). EntryGate and ExitGate are thin: they orchestrate services and hold no policy. PricingStrategy and SpotAllocationStrategy are interfaces. DisplayBoard subscribes to free-count changes.

Patterns and why. Strategy, twice, at exactly the two seams interviewers mutate: pricing (hourly, day-cap, weekend surge, vehicle-type multiplier as a decorator over a base strategy) and allocation (nearest-first now, load-balancing-across-floors later). Gates call the interface; policy swaps never touch gate code. Factory method builds Vehicle and Ticket so construction detail stays out of gates. Observer for DisplayBoard so the allocator never knows rendering exists. Deliberately no Singleton: construct one ParkingLot in main and inject it — same one-instance guarantee, but testable and free of hidden global state; saying this out loud scores.

Key interfaces:
class SpotAllocationStrategy { public: virtual std::optional<SpotId> allocate(VehicleType v) = 0; virtual void release(SpotId s) = 0; virtual ~SpotAllocationStrategy() = default; };
class PricingStrategy { public: virtual Money fee(const Ticket& t, TimePoint exitTime) const = 0; virtual ~PricingStrategy() = default; };
The allocator internally keeps, per (floor, spotType), a std::set<SpotId, ByDistanceFromGate> free index — an ordered container so nearest-first is simply *begin().

Core flow, entry to exit. 1) Vehicle arrives at EntryGate; gate calls allocator->allocate(car). 2) Allocator walks the compatibility row for car — compact first, then large — and per floor in gate-proximity order checks the (floor,type) free index; the first non-empty set yields *begin(). 3) Under that index's lock it erases the id from the set and stores OCCUPIED into the spot's state — one critical section, so index and state can never disagree. 4) Ticket is created (id from a std::atomic<uint64_t> counter) and inserted into the registry; boards are notified with the decremented count. 5) On exit, the gate looks up the ticket, fee = pricing->fee(ticket, now()), payment runs, then release: state -> FREE, reinsert into the index under the same lock, registry erase, boards notified. If payment must precede release, keep the spot OCCUPIED until payment returns — the spot is the resource, the ticket is the claim check.

Concurrency. The only race that matters: two gates, one compatible spot left. Correctness requires the free index and the spot state to change atomically together, so lock at (floor, spotType) granularity — striped locking. Gates on different floors never contend, and even same-floor contention is one erase plus one store long. A lock-free CAS on spot state alone is a trap answer: the winner still must remove the id from the index, so you reacquire ordering anyway; striped locks are the honest mechanism. Ticket ids and revenue counters are atomics. Display counts can be relaxed atomics read without the lock — a stale display is harmless, a misallocated spot is not; say that sentence. TicketRegistry gets its own mutex or a sharded map; it is off the allocation critical path. Deadlock cannot arise because no code path holds two index locks at once — allocate touches one index at a time and release touches exactly one; keep that as a stated invariant when you later add features like in-lot spot upgrades.
lld.cold_prompt: Design a parking lot: 3 floors; motorcycles, cars, trucks; multiple concurrent entry/exit gates; ticket at entry; pay-by-duration at exit; nearest-spot allocation; per-floor free-count displays. Produce from a blank sheet: the entity list with relationships, the two Strategy seams and why they are there, the entry flow end to end, and the exact locking scheme that prevents double allocation. 35 minutes, C++ class sketches expected.
lld.change_request: Mid-design the interviewer adds: EV spots with chargers billed per kWh on top of duration, and monthly-pass vehicles that skip payment at exit. Correct response: EV is a new SpotType row in the compatibility table plus a PricingStrategy decorator that adds energy charges from the charger meter; monthly pass is a check at the exit gate against a PassRegistry before invoking pricing. Zero changes to allocator or gate orchestration. If your pricing was an if-else inside the gate, this is where the design dies — and the interviewer knows it.
lld.failure_injection: The payment processor at the exit gates dies during peak exit. If gates hard-block on payment, the lot physically gridlocks — cars cannot leave, entries back up. Degrade: the exit gate journals ticketId + exitTime to a local append-only log, opens the gate, and bills later from the journal; spot release proceeds normally so entries keep flowing. Bounded revenue risk beats a gridlocked garage. Follow-up you must survive: what if the journal disk fills — cap it, alert at a watermark, and only then fall back to manual/cash lane, never to blocking every gate silently.
tradeoffs: Singleton lot vs dependency injection: inject one instance constructed in main — identical single-instance guarantee, testable, no hidden global state.
Global allocation lock vs per-(floor,type) striped locks: striped — gates on different floors never contend; a global lock serializes every entry and exit in the building.
Assign spot at entry vs when the driver parks: at entry, ticket carries spotId — simpler correctness story; on-demand assignment wastes gate throughput and needs in-lot guidance hardware.
Class hierarchy per spot/vehicle type vs enums plus compatibility table: enums plus table — the variation is data-only; a hierarchy buys downcasts and zero behavior.
Exact real-time display counts vs relaxed atomic counters: relaxed atomics read lock-free — display staleness is harmless, contending on the allocation lock to render a sign is not.

qa.q: Two entry gates race for the last free compact spot. Walk the exact mechanism that prevents both from getting it.
qa.a: The per-(floor,type) free index (std::set of spot ids ordered by distance) and the spot's state change inside one critical section guarded by that index's mutex: lock, erase *begin() from the set, store OCCUPIED, unlock. The losing gate finds the set empty and falls through to the next compatible spot type or floor. A CAS on the spot's atomic state alone is insufficient — the winner must also remove the id from the index, and if state and index can diverge, a gate can be handed an id whose spot is already taken.

qa.q: Why an enum SpotType with a compatibility table instead of a ParkingSpot class hierarchy?
qa.a: Spots differ by data (size class, price multiplier), not behavior — there is nothing to virtually dispatch. The vehicle-to-allowed-spot-types table lives in the allocator, so adding EV or handicapped is a table row plus config; a hierarchy would force downcasts at allocation time for zero gain. Reserve inheritance for genuinely divergent behavior: PricingStrategy and SpotAllocationStrategy, which are the seams interviewers actually mutate.

lld.title: LRU Cache (design + working C++ sketch)
lld.cold_prompt: From a blank file, implement an LRU cache: template over K and V, O(1) get and put, correct eviction order, no dangling map entries. Then make it thread-safe, state why a readers-writer lock does not help, and describe how you scale it under contention. Compile-worthy C++ in 20 minutes, no references handed out from the cache.
lld.change_request: Mid-design: every entry now gets a TTL — get must never return an expired value, and expired entries should stop occupying capacity. Correct response: store an expiry timestamp in each node; get checks it, and on expiry erases the node and reports a miss (lazy expiry); put, before evicting the LRU tail for capacity, first sweeps expired nodes so live data is not evicted to protect dead data. A timer thread per entry is the wrong answer — thousands of timers, wakeup storms, and races against get; lazy checks plus an optional coarse periodic sweep achieve the same correctness for free.
lld.failure_injection: The database this cache fronts goes down exactly as a hot key expires: ten thousand threads miss simultaneously and stampede a dead backend — cache stampede. Fix: request coalescing — a per-key inflight map of shared futures under the cache mutex, so exactly one loader runs and 9,999 callers wait on its future; the loader's result (or failure) resolves all of them. Layer on stale-while-revalidate if you retain expired values, and negative-cache the failure with a short TTL so retries do not hammer a corpse. Be ready to sketch the inflight-futures mechanism: find-or-emplace future, only the emplacer loads, everyone else waits.
tradeoffs: std::list + unordered_map vs hand-rolled intrusive list: std::list first — splice gives O(1) moves with stable iterators; hand-roll only when profiling proves allocator overhead, then intrusive nodes with a pool.
Single mutex vs sharding: single mutex until contention is measured; then shard by key hash into N caches of C/N — accept per-shard rather than global LRU order, exactly as memcached does.
Return reference vs value/shared_ptr: value or shared_ptr<V> — a returned reference can dangle the instant another thread triggers eviction.
shared_mutex vs plain mutex: plain mutex — get() splices, so there are no pure readers and shared locking degenerates to exclusive anyway.
Strict LRU vs approximations (CLOCK, sampled eviction): strict for the interview implementation; volunteer that Redis samples ~5 keys for approximate LRU to eliminate list maintenance at scale.

qa.q: Why must the linked-list node store the key, and what exactly breaks without it?
qa.a: Eviction traverses list -> map: pop_back hands you the tail node, and you need its key to erase the corresponding map entry. Without the key in the node, the map entry for the evicted key survives holding an iterator into a destroyed list node — the next get(evictedKey) finds it, dereferences a dangling iterator, and is undefined behavior. Storing the key in the pair is the entire reason the structure is coherent in both directions.

qa.q: Why does std::shared_mutex not help LRU get(), and what is the correct scaling move?
qa.a: get() splices the accessed node to the list front — it mutates shared structure, so every get is a writer and must take the exclusive lock; shared_mutex adds overhead and zero parallelism. The correct move is sharding: N independent caches of capacity C/N, key routed by hash(k) % N, each with its own mutex. Contention divides by N; the cost is LRU ordering per shard instead of globally, which real systems accept.

lld.title: Rate Limiter (token bucket + sliding window, thread-safe)
lld.cold_prompt: Design a rate limiter library from a blank sheet: bool allow(clientKey), pluggable algorithms (token bucket and sliding window — both), thread-safe, millions of client keys. Produce: the interface, each algorithm's per-key state and update math, the two-layer locking scheme (key map and per-key state), the clock choice with justification, and stale-key eviction. 30 minutes, C++ sketch for the token bucket.
lld.change_request: Mid-design: take it distributed — 20 API servers must jointly enforce 100 rps per client. Options to present: central Redis bucket with a Lua script for atomic read-modify-write (exact, adds a network hop per request, Redis becomes a dependency), versus local buckets at limit/N per node (zero added latency, drifts when traffic skews across nodes). Right call: Redis+Lua for correctness on billable/abuse-sensitive limits, with a local fallback bucket for when Redis is unreachable; local-only is acceptable when limits are advisory. State the consistency-versus-latency axis explicitly.
lld.failure_injection: Redis — the shared limiter state — becomes unreachable. Fail-open means an abuser gets unlimited throughput; fail-closed means a self-inflicted outage for every client. The answer is per-endpoint policy, not one global switch: fail-open on cheap idempotent reads, fail-closed or degrade to a local fallback bucket at limit/N per node on expensive or abuse-prone writes; alert loudly either way. Justify the limit/N error band: worst case a client concentrated on one node gets limit/N (under-serving), spread across all nodes gets up to limit (correct) — bounded, predictable, and temporary, which is what you want from a fallback.
tradeoffs: Token bucket vs sliding-window log: token bucket — O(1) state per key (tokens + timestamp) and burst as a first-class parameter; the log is exact but stores O(limit) timestamps per key.
Sliding-window counter vs log: counter at scale — the weighted two-window estimate is O(1) memory with small boundary error; exactness rarely justifies the log's memory.
Lazy refill vs background refill thread: lazy — refill computed from elapsed time on each request; no timer thread, no wakeups, identical correctness.
Per-key mutex with sharded map vs global mutex: sharded with per-bucket locks — a global mutex serializes all clients; a hot key should only serialize with itself.
Fail-open vs fail-closed on state-store failure: neither globally — per-endpoint policy, fail-open for cheap reads, fail-closed or local limit/N fallback for expensive writes.

qa.q: An API gateway needs bursts of 100 with a sustained 10 rps per client. Which algorithm, and what is the per-key memory cost of each option?
qa.a: Token bucket with capacity 100 and refill 10 tokens/sec — burst is a native parameter, and per-key state is O(1): one double (tokens) plus one timestamp. Sliding-window log would be exact but stores up to 100 timestamps per key (O(limit)); sliding-window counter is O(1) (two counters + window index) but only approximates burst behavior at window boundaries. When burst semantics are in the requirement, token bucket is the direct encoding of it.

qa.q: Why steady_clock instead of system_clock, and what is the companion clamp that keeps refill honest?
qa.a: system_clock tracks wall time, which jumps backward under NTP correction or manual changes — a backward jump makes elapsed negative or a forward jump enormous, minting free tokens and corrupting window eviction. steady_clock is monotonic: differences between two readings are always non-negative and meaningful. The companion is tokens = min(capacity, tokens + elapsed*rate) — the clamp prevents an idle key from banking unbounded credit while it slept.

lld.title: Splitwise (expense splitting + debt simplification)
lld.worked:
Requirements. Users; groups; addExpense(payer, amount, participants, splitType) supporting equal, exact, and percent splits; per-pair balances; showBalance(user); settle-up recording; simplifyDebts within a group. Money must be exact to the smallest unit; every change must be auditable. The evaluation axes: split-strategy design, money representation, the settlement algorithm and its guarantee, and write-path integrity.

Entities. User. Group (members, expense ids). Expense { id, payerId, Money amount, vector<Split>, timestamp, description } as an immutable record. Split { userId, Money share }, produced by a SplitStrategy. BalanceGraph — net pairwise balances, a derived projection of the ledger. ExpenseService orchestrating validation, ledger append, and balance update. NotificationService observing expense events.

Money is int64 minor units (paise), full stop. Never double: 0.1 + 0.2 style errors compound across thousands of splits into off-by-a-paisa balances that users screenshot. Equal split of 1000 paise among 3 uses the largest-remainder method: base 333 each, the leftover 1 paisa assigned deterministically (first participant by sorted user id), so shares always sum exactly to the amount and any recomputation agrees forever — determinism is part of correctness here, not style.

Patterns and why. Strategy for splits: EqualSplit, ExactSplit, PercentSplit each implement computeShares(amount, participants, params) and validate() — exact shares must sum to the amount, percentages (kept as integer basis points) must sum to 10000. Validation lives inside each strategy so Expense stays a dumb record and adding a weighted-shares split is one new class. A factory maps the request enum to the strategy. Observer for notifications. The deepest decision is the ledger: append-only. An edit or delete is a compensating entry referencing the original, never a mutation. Balances are a projection you can rebuild by replaying the ledger, which turns money-bug debugging from archaeology into a deterministic replay — say the word replay, it lands.

Balance model. Net amount per canonical ordered pair: map<pair<UserId,UserId>, int64> with smaller id first and a signed value, or equivalently per-user nets within a group. Applying an expense: for each participant other than the payer, edge(participant -> payer) += share. showBalance(u) walks the edges incident to u.

Debt simplification. Compute net(u) = totalPaid - totalOwed for every member; internal cycles cancel automatically at this step. Drop everyone at net zero. Greedy settlement: a max-heap of creditors and a max-heap of debtors; repeatedly match the largest creditor with the largest debtor, settle min(credit, |debt|), reinsert any nonzero remainder. Each settlement zeroes at least one person, so at most n-1 transactions, O(n log n) total. Then state the honest limit unprompted: minimizing the exact number of transactions is NP-hard — it reduces to finding the maximum number of disjoint zero-sum subsets (subset-sum territory) — so greedy n-1 is the production answer, and exact search is only for toy group sizes. Knowing where optimal stops being worth it scores more than the algorithm itself.

Flow. addExpense: equal, 900 among {A,B,C}, A pays. Service validates membership; EqualSplit yields 300/300/300; the immutable expense appends to the ledger; edges B->A += 300 and C->A += 300; observers fire. Later B settles 300: a Settlement entry appends (also append-only, carrying a client-generated idempotency key) and edge B->A -= 300. simplifyDebts runs on demand: compute nets, run the greedy, present suggested transfers — suggestions never mutate the ledger until a settlement is actually recorded, and say why: simplification rewrites who-owes-whom, and applying it silently on every write churns the graph under users mid-session and breaks in-flight settle-ups.

Concurrency and integrity. Two concurrent addExpense calls in one group are a read-modify-write on shared balances: serialize per group — a per-group mutex or a single-writer queue per group; operations in different groups never contend. Every mutating API takes a client-generated idempotency key backed by a unique index, because mobile clients retry and a double-recorded settlement is the worst bug this system can produce. Ledger append and balance update commit in one transaction; if balances are cached, a mismatch triggers rebuild-from-ledger. One sentence to close every answer in this domain: the ledger is the single source of truth, everything else is a view.
lld.cold_prompt: Design Splitwise from a blank sheet: users, groups, addExpense with equal/exact/percent splits, per-pair balances, showBalance, settle-up, and simplifyDebts. Produce: the entity list, the split Strategy design with validation placement, the money representation with the rounding rule, the append-only ledger argument, and the settlement algorithm with its transaction-count guarantee and complexity. 35 minutes.
lld.change_request: Mid-design: support multi-currency — expenses in USD, INR, EUR; balances displayed in each user's home currency. Correct response: store amount + currency on every ledger entry and never convert at write time; convert at read time using a rate snapshot for display; settle within a currency by default, with cross-currency settlement as an explicit user action at an agreed rate recorded on the settlement entry. Converting at write corrupts the ledger the moment rates move — the stored numbers stop matching any real transaction, and replay stops reproducing reality. The ledger records what happened; presentation does the converting.
lld.failure_injection: The settle-up endpoint double-fires: B pays 500 once, the client times out and retries, and two ledger entries land. Balances are now wrong in the direction that destroys trust. Immediate fix: compensating entry referencing the duplicate — never delete, the audit trail is the product. Systemic fix: every mutating request carries a client-generated idempotency key (uuid) with a unique index; the retry hits the index and returns the original result. Follow-up to survive: detecting existing doubles retroactively — scan for same payer/payee/amount within a tight time window without distinct keys, flag for user confirmation rather than auto-repair.
tradeoffs: Mutable balances vs append-only ledger with derived balances: append-only — edits become compensating entries, audit and deterministic replay come free, and balances are rebuildable views.
double vs int64 minor units for money: integers, always — floating error compounds across splits; largest-remainder distributes leftover paise deterministically so shares sum exactly.
Simplify debts on every write vs on demand: on demand — simplification rewrites the debt graph; doing it per-write churns balances under active users and breaks pending settle-ups.
Greedy max-creditor/max-debtor vs optimal transaction count: greedy — at most n-1 transactions in O(n log n); exact minimization is NP-hard and only viable for toy groups.

Split validation in Expense vs inside each SplitStrategy: inside the strategy — exact-sums-to-total and percent-sums-to-100 live next to the math that defines them; Expense stays an immutable dumb record.

qa.q: Why an append-only ledger with derived balances, and why integer minor units — give the failure each choice prevents.
qa.a: Append-only prevents silent history rewrites: an edit or delete becomes a compensating entry referencing the original, so any balance can be re-derived by replay and money bugs become reproducible instead of archaeological. Integer minor units (paise) prevent floating-point drift: double arithmetic loses fractions of a paisa per split and compounds across thousands of expenses. The companion rule is largest-remainder rounding — 1000/3 gives 334/333/333 with the extra paisa assigned deterministically by sorted user id, so shares always sum exactly to the amount.

qa.q: Does the max-debtor/max-creditor greedy minimize the number of settlement transactions?
qa.a: No. It guarantees at most n-1 transactions among the n users with nonzero net, because each settlement zeroes at least one person, and it runs in O(n log n) with two heaps. The true minimum is n minus the maximum number of disjoint zero-sum subsets of the net vector, and finding that partition is NP-hard (subset-sum reduction). The interview-grade answer: greedy n-1 is the production choice; exact minimization is exponential and only defensible for groups small enough to brute-force.

lld.title: Elevator System (scheduling + state machine)
lld.worked:
