Requirements. N cars, M floors. Hall calls carry a direction (up/down pressed at a floor); car calls carry a destination (pressed inside a car). A dispatcher chooses which car serves each hall call; each car schedules its own stops non-stupidly (not FCFS); every car runs a state machine including door handling; capacity limits and maintenance mode exist. The two graded skills: the LOOK scheduling discipline and a direction-aware dispatch cost function.

Entities. ElevatorController — owns the cars, receives hall calls on a queue, runs dispatch. ElevatorCar { id, currentFloor, Direction, CarState, stop sets }. Door. HallCall { floor, direction }. CarCall { floor }. DispatchStrategy interface. HallPanel per floor and in-car Panel as thin request producers; Displays as observers.

Car scheduling is LOOK: keep moving in the current direction, stopping at every requested floor in that direction, reverse only when nothing remains ahead. Implementation: stopsAbove as std::set<int> ascending and stopsBelow as std::set<int> descending (or one set plus direction logic). Moving up, the next target is *stopsAbove.begin(); when that set empties, flip direction and drain the other. A car call inserts into the set matching its position relative to currentFloor. An assigned hall call inserts the same way but keeps its direction tag, so a car heading up does not open for a down-waiting passenger at an intermediate floor — serving them would only re-serve them after reversal anyway.

State machine. States: Idle, MovingUp, MovingDown, DoorsOpen, OutOfService. Legal transitions: Idle -> Moving on assignment; Moving -> DoorsOpen on arrival at a stop; DoorsOpen -> Moving or Idle after the door cycle; any state -> OutOfService via maintenance. Start with an enum plus one explicit transition function — a table you can unit-test exhaustively; graduate to the State pattern when per-state behavior accumulates (DoorsOpen must reject motion commands, OutOfService must drain its queue and hand hall calls back). Saying enum-first, pattern-when-earned reads as judgment rather than pattern-dropping, and interviewers reward it.

Dispatch. Strategy interface score(car, hallCall) -> cost; the controller assigns the minimum-cost InService car. Nearest-car is the naive baseline and fails on direction: a car at floor 4 moving up must not win a down call at floor 3. Direction-aware cost: same direction with the call ahead -> cost = |distance|; idle -> distance plus a small tiebreak penalty; opposite direction or call behind -> distance to the reversal point plus travel back. Add a load penalty near capacity and an aging bonus so far-floor calls cannot starve under a lobby storm. Worked example to rehearse: car A at 2 moving up with a stop at 7; car B idle at 8; hall-down at 5. A serves 5 only after 7 and reversal, cost about (7-2)+(7-5)=7; B costs 3 plus idle penalty — assign B. If you propose nearest-car, the interviewer runs exactly this case at you.

Flow of one hall call. Down pressed at floor 5 -> HallCall{5,down} lands on the controller's thread-safe queue -> dispatch loop pops it, scores all InService cars, assigns the minimum -> that car inserts 5 with a down tag -> the car's tick loop advances: Moving, arrival at 5, transition to DoorsOpen, stop cleared, controller notified served -> the boarding passenger presses 1, CarCall inserts into stopsBelow -> doors close, direction is now down, LOOK continues.

Patterns summary: Strategy for dispatch, State (or disciplined enum machine) for the car, Observer for displays, Command in the sense that requests are queued objects — which is what decouples button hardware from scheduling.

Concurrency. The interview-honest topology is a single controller tick loop: deterministic, unit-testable, and lock-free beyond the inbound queue. The threaded variant — a thread per car plus the controller — changes nothing structurally: each car's stop sets get a mutex, and assignment becomes message passing; say that sentence and stay with the tick loop. The genuinely dangerous shared state is assignment bookkeeping: the controller must retain which hall calls it handed to which car, because when a car drops to OutOfService those hall calls must return to the pool for re-dispatch. Car calls cannot be re-dispatched — those passengers are physically inside the failed car; that is the alarm-and-rescue path, not a scheduling path, and distinguishing the two is the mark of a candidate who actually simulated the system in their head.
lld.cold_prompt: Design an elevator system from a blank sheet: N cars, M floors, hall calls with direction plus car calls, LOOK scheduling with the exact data structures, the car state machine with legal transitions, and a direction-aware dispatch cost function. Walk one hall call end to end including direction reversal, and state where assignment bookkeeping lives and why. 35 minutes.
lld.change_request: Mid-design: switch to destination dispatch — passengers enter their target floor at a lobby kiosk; there are no up/down buttons and no in-car panel step. Model change: HallCall becomes Request { originFloor, destinationFloor }, which is strictly more information; the two-phase hall-then-car flow collapses to one. Dispatch improves because the controller can batch requests with similar destination bands onto the same car (grouping 30-35 riders together), cutting stops per trip. The car's LOOK machinery is unchanged — only the request model and the cost function (now scoring origin pickup plus destination fit) evolve. Show that the design absorbs this as a new DispatchStrategy plus a Request type, not a rewrite.
lld.failure_injection: Car 2 fails hard at floor 6 with four queued stops and two assigned hall calls. Required controller behavior: detect via missed heartbeat or tick timeout; mark the car OutOfService; return its two assigned hall calls to the global pool for re-dispatch to healthy cars; raise the trapped-passenger alarm for its car calls — those cannot be re-dispatched because the requesters are inside the failed car. The trap in the question: if assignment state lived only inside the car, the controller cannot recover the hall calls after the car dies — which is exactly why the controller retains an assignment map. If you put all state in the car, you fail this injection by construction.
tradeoffs: Central controller vs fully autonomous cars: central dispatcher assigning hall calls, car owning its stop queue — one place to change dispatch policy and no consensus problem inside a single process.
FCFS vs LOOK per car: LOOK — serve the current direction to exhaustion then reverse; FCFS thrashes the car across floors and is the canonical wrong answer.

State pattern vs enum + transition function: enum with an explicit, testable transition table first; move to State objects only when per-state behavior (door interlocks, maintenance draining) accumulates.
Nearest-car dispatch vs direction-aware cost: direction-aware — same-direction-ahead beats raw proximity; nearest-car sends an up-moving car to a down call it cannot serve until reversal.
Thread-per-car vs single tick loop: single tick loop for the interview — deterministic and unit-testable; threading adds a mutex per car and a queue, buying realism but zero design change.

qa.q: A car at floor 3 is moving up to serve a stop at 7. A hall-down call arrives at floor 5. Under LOOK, when does that car serve it, and what does this imply for the dispatch cost function?
qa.a: Not on the way up — the call's direction (down) mismatches the car's travel direction, so stopping at 5 now would strand the passenger or force an out-of-order reversal. The car finishes upward work (7), reverses, and serves 5 heading down: effective cost (7-3)+(7-5)=6, not 2. Dispatch must therefore score direction: same-direction-ahead = distance; idle = distance + small penalty; opposite/behind = distance to reversal point plus return — so an idle car at 8 (cost 3 + penalty) correctly wins this call.

qa.q: A car goes OutOfService holding assigned hall calls and car calls. What happens to each, and what state must the controller have kept?
qa.a: Hall calls return to the controller's pool and are re-dispatched to healthy cars — the waiting passengers are still on their floors. Car calls cannot be re-dispatched: those passengers are inside the failed car, so that path is trapped-passenger alarm and rescue, not scheduling. This works only if the controller retained an assignment map (hallCall -> carId) rather than pushing all state into the car; detection comes from a missed heartbeat or tick timeout, after which the car is excluded from scoring until maintenance clears it.

lld.title: Movie Ticket Booking (seat locking + concurrency)
lld.worked:
Requirements. Catalog: city -> cinema -> screen -> show. Seat map per show; a user selects seats, the seats are held with a TTL (say 10 minutes), payment runs, then confirmation. Zero double bookings even with multiple app servers. Holds expire and release automatically. Bookings have an explicit lifecycle. The graded core is the locking protocol and the payment edge cases; the graded modeling decision is ShowSeat.

Entities. Movie; Cinema (belongs to a city); Screen (owns physical Seats); Seat { id, row, type }; Show { id, screenId, movieId, startTime }; ShowSeat { showId, seatId, status FREE/HELD/BOOKED, holdOwner, holdExpiry, version } — the single most important modeling decision: status lives on ShowSeat, not Seat, because the same physical seat is FREE for the 6pm show and BOOKED for the 9pm show. Candidates who hang status on Seat fail the question in the first five minutes. Booking { id, userId, showId, seatIds, amount, state CREATED/PENDING_PAYMENT/CONFIRMED/EXPIRED/CANCELLED }; SeatLockManager behind an interface; PaymentService; a pricing strategy over seat type and show time.

Patterns. State for the booking lifecycle with explicit legal transitions (CREATED -> PENDING_PAYMENT -> CONFIRMED; CREATED or PENDING_PAYMENT -> EXPIRED; CONFIRMED -> CANCELLED/REFUNDED) — illegal transitions throw, which is what makes webhook races safe. Strategy for pricing. The lock manager interface lets an in-memory implementation (single node, tests) swap for the database/Redis implementation (production) without touching booking code.

The locking protocol, end to end. Hold: lockSeats(showId, seatIds, userId) — take the per-show stripe lock (never a global one; shows are independent); for every requested seat verify status is FREE, or HELD with holdExpiry < now (expired holds are reclaimable); if any seat fails, acquire nothing and return the exact conflict set — all-or-nothing, because partial holds strand couples and invite deadlock; otherwise write HELD + owner + expiry = now + TTL for all seats and release the stripe. Sorting seat ids before any multi-lock acquisition is the classic deadlock guard; with a single stripe per show it is belt-and-braces, and saying so shows you know why it exists. Confirm: after payment success, under the same stripe verify every seat is still HELD by this user, flip all to BOOKED, booking to CONFIRMED. Expiry: lazy everywhere — every read treats an expired HELD as FREE — plus a periodic sweep that clears expired holds and notifies any waitlist; lazy gives correctness, the sweep gives memory back and UX signals.

Multiple app servers make in-process mutexes a latency optimization, not a correctness mechanism: the authority is the shared store. Pessimistic SQL: a transaction doing SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on the ShowSeat rows, check, then update. Or a conditional write: UPDATE show_seat SET status=HELD, owner=X, expiry=Y WHERE show_id=S AND seat_id IN (...) AND (status=FREE OR (status=HELD AND expiry<now)) — commit only if affected rows equals the seat count, else roll back. Last line of defense either way: a unique partial index over (show_id, seat_id) for BOOKED rows, so even a logic bug cannot double-book durably. Redis variant for the hold layer: SET lock:{show}:{seat} owner NX PX ttl per seat, with rollback of acquired keys on partial failure — and note that money rides on the database, Redis is the latency tier.

The walkthrough interviewers want verbatim: users A and B both want seats 5 and 6 of show S, hitting different app servers. Both servers issue the conditional update; the database serializes them; A's update matches 2 rows and commits, B's matches 0, rolls back, and B receives the precise conflict list plus a refreshed seat map. No sleeping and retrying, no trusting in-memory state across nodes.

Payment is where designs die, so pre-commit to three cases. Payment is slow and external, so it runs outside any lock, between hold and confirm, with the booking in PENDING_PAYMENT and the bookingId as the idempotency key sent to the gateway. Webhook arrives twice: confirm is idempotent — the second call finds CONFIRMED and no-ops. Webhook arrives late: the hold TTL must exceed the gateway's maximum timeout, so a slow success still finds its seats HELD. Webhook never arrives: a reconciliation job polls the gateway for the payment intent's status before the sweep may free a PENDING_PAYMENT hold. Read path closes the design: the browsing seat map is served from cache and may be seconds stale — correctness lives entirely in the hold/confirm path, and hardening the read path buys nothing because the map is stale the moment it renders.
lld.cold_prompt: Design the booking core of a BookMyShow-style system from a blank sheet: city -> cinema -> screen -> show -> seats; seat map; hold-then-pay with a 10-minute TTL; zero double bookings across multiple app servers. Produce: the entity model (get ShowSeat right and say why), the full locking protocol from hold to confirm including expiry, the booking state machine, and the three payment-webhook edge cases with their handling. 40 minutes.
lld.change_request: Mid-design: a blockbuster opens and 500k users hit one show's seat map in the first minute. Correct response: add a virtual waiting room — a queue token issued before the seat map is reachable, admitting users in batches sized to what the hold path can absorb; serve the browsing seat map from cache with eventual accuracy; keep strict locking only on the hold/confirm path, which now sees bounded traffic. Explicitly refuse to make the seat map strongly consistent: it is stale the instant it renders, users tolerate a hold-time conflict message, and burning the database to keep a display honest is spending correctness budget where it buys nothing.
lld.failure_injection: The payment gateway charges the user, but the success webhook is lost and the seat hold TTL expires. The sweep frees the seats, another user books them, and the first user was charged for nothing — the worst outcome the system can produce. Prevention: holds attached to a booking in PENDING_PAYMENT are exempt from the sweep until the gateway has been polled for the payment intent's terminal status; the TTL only hard-expires holds with no payment in flight. Backstop: a reconciliation job matches gateway settlements against bookings and triggers auto-refund plus compensation for any charged-but-lost case. Design the sweep's decision table: (no booking) free; (CREATED, expired) free; (PENDING_PAYMENT) poll gateway first — free only on failed/expired intent, confirm on succeeded; (CONFIRMED) never touch.
tradeoffs: Optimistic version-check at confirm vs pessimistic hold with TTL: pessimistic hold — users will not tolerate paying and then losing the seat on a hot show; optimistic is acceptable only for cold inventory.

In-process lock manager vs shared-store authority: the database (FOR UPDATE or conditional update + unique partial index on booked rows) is the authority across app servers; in-process locks are a latency optimization only.
Status on Seat vs ShowSeat: ShowSeat — one physical seat has independent status per show; putting status on Seat is the canonical modeling failure for this question.
All-or-nothing seat holds vs partial holds: all-or-nothing with seat ids sorted before acquisition — partial holds strand adjacent-seat groups and create deadlock windows.
Sweep-based expiry vs lazy expiry checks: both — lazy check on every read is the cheap correctness guarantee; the periodic sweep reclaims memory and feeds waitlist notifications.

qa.q: Give the exact mechanism that prevents double booking of seat S for show X when requests land on different app servers.
qa.a: In-process locks cannot arbitrate across servers, so the shared store is the authority. Either pessimistic — a transaction with SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on the ShowSeat rows, verify FREE-or-expired-HELD, then update — or a single conditional UPDATE setting HELD with WHERE status=FREE OR (status=HELD AND expiry<now), committing only if affected rows equals the requested seat count. The database serializes the two servers; the loser matches 0 rows and gets the conflict list. Final backstop: a unique partial index on (show_id, seat_id) for BOOKED rows makes durable double booking impossible even under a logic bug.

qa.q: Payment succeeded, the webhook was lost, the hold expired, and the seats were resold. How do you prevent this, and failing that, resolve it?
qa.a: Prevent: set hold TTL greater than the gateway's maximum timeout, and make the expiry sweep treat PENDING_PAYMENT holds as untouchable until it polls the gateway for the payment intent's terminal status — free only on a failed/expired intent, confirm the booking on a succeeded one. Resolve: a reconciliation job matches gateway settlements to booking states and auto-refunds (plus compensates) any charged-but-unconfirmed user. Every webhook and confirm call is idempotent, keyed by bookingId, so duplicates and replays during recovery are no-ops.

hld.title: URL Shortener (TinyURL)
hld.worked: REQUIREMENTS. Functional: create a short code for a long URL, redirect on access, optional custom alias, optional expiry, delete. Non-functional: redirect p99 under 50 ms, very high availability on the read path (a broken redirect is a broken product), read:write around 100:1, codes must not be trivially enumerable. ESTIMATION. Assume 100M new URLs per month. Write QPS: 100e6 / (30 x 86400) = 100e6 / 2.6e6 s = ~40 writes/sec, peak ~100. Read QPS: 100:1 ratio -> 4,000/sec average, 3x peak = ~12,000/sec. Storage: retain 5 years -> 100M x 60 months = 6B rows; ~500 bytes per row (7-byte code, ~300-byte URL, user id, timestamps) -> 6e9 x 500B = 3 TB total. That is small: this is a key-value problem, not a big-data problem. Bandwidth: 4,000/s x 500B = 2 MB/s on the read path - negligible. Cache sizing: 4,000/s x 86400 = ~350M redirects/day; 80/20 rule -> cache the hot 20% of targets, ~70M entries x 500B = ~35 GB -> a small Redis cluster. API. POST /api/v1/urls {longUrl, customAlias?, expiresAt?} -> {shortUrl}; authenticated, rate limited. GET /{code} -> 302 redirect, 404 unknown, 410 expired. DELETE /api/v1/urls/{code}. Custom aliases live in the same keyspace: put-if-absent on the requested alias, 409 on conflict, and reserve a length/prefix namespace so aliases can never collide with generated codes. DATA MODEL. One table: code (PK, char(7) base62), long_url, user_id, created_at, expires_at. Zero joins -> key-value store (DynamoDB/Cassandra) or sharded MySQL keyed on code. COMPONENTS. Client -> LB -> stateless read service (redirect) and write service (creation), split so the read path scales independently -> Redis cache-aside -> DB. Read flow end to end: LB -> read server -> in-process LRU -> Redis -> DB -> backfill both caches -> 302; a total miss costs two network hops and stays inside 10 ms. DEEP DIVE: KEY GENERATION. Base62 alphabet, 7 chars = 62^7 = ~3.5 trillion codes - centuries of headroom at 1.2B/year. Option 1, hash the URL (MD5, take 42 bits -> 7 base62 chars): collisions are rare but real, so every insert needs put-if-absent plus a salted re-hash retry on conflict; identical URLs also collide across users unless salted, which may or may not be desired. Option 2, global counter encoded in base62: collision-free by construction, but a single point of failure and sequential codes are enumerable. Fix: each app server leases a range of 1M ids from a coordinator (ZooKeeper, or a DB row with atomic increment), serves ids from memory, and a crashed server simply burns its unused range - irrelevant against 3.5T capacity. Apply a bijective bit-permutation to the counter before encoding so codes look random. Option 3, a Key Generation Service: an offline job pre-fills a pool of random unused keys and app servers grab batches. Interview answer: range-leased counter with bit mixing, or KGS - both keep collision checks off the hot write path. Second decision: 301 vs 302. A 301 is cached permanently by browsers so repeat clicks never reach you - cheapest, but you lose all analytics and can never repoint the link. A 302 keeps every click observable. Pick 302 when analytics matter, which is almost always. BOTTLENECKS AND SCALING. Hot key: one viral link concentrates on one cache entry -> a tiny in-process LRU on each read server (even a 1 s TTL absorbs the spike) or replicate hot entries across cache nodes. Cache stampede when a hot entry expires: request coalescing (single-flight) or probabilistic early refresh. DB scaling: shard by hash(code); every redirect is a single-shard point read. Availability: entries are immutable after creation, so replication is trivial - no update conflicts - run multi-region read replicas and serve stale-tolerant reads anywhere. Analytics: never touch the redirect path synchronously; emit a click event to Kafka and aggregate downstream. Expiry: lazy check on read plus a background sweeper. Abuse: per-key creation rate limits and a malware/phishing blocklist checked at creation and re-scanned asynchronously.
hld.cold_prompt: Design a URL shortener like TinyURL for 100M new links/month with a 100:1 read:write ratio. From a blank sheet, cover: estimation showing arithmetic (write QPS, read QPS, 5-year storage, cache size), the API, the data model, your key-generation scheme with its collision story and anti-enumeration measure, the 301-vs-302 decision, cache strategy for the redirect path, and how redirects survive DB trouble.
hld.change_request: Mid-design change: add per-link analytics - total clicks, unique visitors, and a near-real-time top-100 dashboard - without adding a single millisecond to the redirect path. Where do events flow, what aggregates them, and what does this do to your 301-vs-302 choice?
hld.failure_injection: The entire Redis cache tier goes down at peak load of 12,000 redirects/sec. The DB was sized assuming an 80%+ cache hit rate. What happens in the first 30 seconds, what do you do in the next 5 minutes, and what do you build next sprint so this never pages anyone again?
tradeoffs: 301 vs 302 redirect: choose 302 - keeps every click observable for analytics and lets targets be updated; you give up the browser-cache load offload of 301.

Hash vs counter-range vs KGS for code generation: choose range-leased counter (or KGS) - collision-free hot path; accept burned id ranges on server crash and add a bit-permutation to defeat enumeration.
SQL vs key-value store: choose KV (DynamoDB/Cassandra) - single-key point reads, no joins, effortless sharding; give up ad-hoc queries and do those in the analytics pipeline instead.
Cache-aside vs write-through caching: choose cache-aside - most newly created links are never read, so write-through wastes cache on cold entries.
Keep-forever vs default expiry: choose default expiry with opt-in permanence - bounds storage growth and the abuse surface at trivial product cost.

qa.q: Why 7 base62 characters and not 6?
qa.a: 62^7 = ~3.5 x 10^12 codes; at 100M/month = 1.2B/year that is nearly 3,000 years of headroom. 62^6 = ~5.7 x 10^10 gives only ~47 years - workable, but 7 chars removes the question permanently for one extra character, and short-code UX is unaffected.

qa.q: Your counter-based codes are sequential - how do you stop someone crawling /aaaaaab, /aaaaaac and scraping every private link?
qa.a: Never encode the raw counter. Apply a bijective bit-mix (e.g. a fixed random permutation of the 42 bits, or multiply by an odd constant mod 2^42) before base62 encoding - still collision-free, looks random. Alternatively pre-generate random keys via a KGS. Also rate-limit high-404 clients as a probe signal.

qa.q: Exact collision handling if you generate codes by hashing the URL?
qa.a: Insert with a conditional put-if-absent on the code key (DynamoDB conditional write / INSERT IGNORE + check). On conflict where the stored long_url differs, append a salt or retry counter to the input and re-hash; bound retries (2-3 suffice, collisions at 42 bits are rare). Counter/KGS schemes make collisions impossible by construction, which is exactly why they win.

hld.title: Distributed Rate Limiter
hld.worked: REQUIREMENTS. Enforce per-client limits (user id, API key, or IP) such as 100 req/min, with per-endpoint rule configs; return 429 with Retry-After and X-RateLimit-* headers; add well under 1 ms to the request path; enforce correctly across a fleet of gateway nodes, not per-process; define behavior when the limiter itself fails (fail-open vs fail-closed); slight inaccuracy is acceptable, silently throttling legitimate traffic is not. ESTIMATION. Gateway fleet at 100k QPS aggregate means the limiter performs 100k decisions/sec - it sits on every request, so its cost is the product's cost. Distinct active clients: 10M/day. State per client with a sliding-window counter: two counters plus TTL, ~100 bytes -> 10M x 100B = 1 GB total - one modest Redis cluster. Contrast with a sliding-window LOG (one timestamp per request): 10M clients x up to 100 timestamps x 8B = ~8 GB, plus O(window) work per decision - this arithmetic is the argument for counters. Network: one Redis round trip per decision at 100k QPS; colocate Redis in the same AZ so RTT is ~0.3 ms and the latency budget survives. Rules config: thousands of rules x a few hundred bytes - trivially cached in every gateway's memory. ALGORITHM MENU. Fixed window: INCR on key client:rule:window_start with EXPIRE - cheapest and natively atomic, but allows up to 2x the limit straddling a window boundary. Sliding window log: exact but expensive - only for tiny, high-value limits. Sliding window counter: keep current and previous window counts; estimate = curr + prev x (1 - elapsed fraction of current window); O(1) memory, near-exact - the default choice. Token bucket: capacity plus refill rate, two variables per key; permits controlled bursts, which matches how clients actually behave - the choice when the requirement says bursts are legitimate. Leaky bucket: smooths egress to a constant rate - for protecting downstreams that need steady input. API. Gateway middleware: check(client_id, rule_id) -> allow or deny(retry_after). On deny: HTTP 429 with Retry-After and X-RateLimit-Limit/Remaining/Reset. Rules live in a config service, cached in gateway memory, hot-reloaded on change. DATA MODEL. One Redis entry per (client, rule): token bucket stores tokens and last_refill_ts; sliding counter stores curr and prev. TTL = two windows so idle clients cost nothing. Shard by client id across the cluster. COMPONENTS. Client -> LB -> gateway (limiter middleware -> local rules cache -> Redis cluster) -> backend services; config service feeds rules; a metrics pipeline consumes allow/deny events. DEEP DIVE: ATOMICITY AND CLOCKS. The naive sequence GET tokens, decide, SET tokens is a read-modify-write race: two gateways read tokens=1 concurrently, both admit, limit exceeded. Fix: execute the entire decision as a Redis Lua script - Redis is single-threaded, so the script runs atomically: elapsed = now - last_refill; tokens = min(capacity, tokens + elapsed x refill_rate); if tokens >= 1, decrement and allow, else deny. Take now from the Redis TIME command inside the script, never from gateway clocks - that deletes inter-node clock skew from the design entirely. Hot shard: one giant customer concentrates load on one key; split their global limit into per-gateway sub-limits that sum to the total (accepting unevenness under skew), or pin them to a dedicated shard. Sub-millisecond variant: when even 0.3 ms is too much, enforce locally in each gateway from an in-memory bucket sized limit/N for N gateways and reconcile counts asynchronously - fast but inaccurate when traffic skews across gateways; state that tradeoff out loud. BOTTLENECKS AND SCALING. Store outage: fail-open (admit everything - users happy, backends exposed) versus fail-closed (deny everything - backends safe, users furious). Right call for user-facing APIs: fail-open, degraded to a conservative in-process local limiter so backends keep some protection; fail-closed only in front of fragile or expensive downstreams like a payments processor. Multi-region: enforce within each region - clients are region-sticky - and reconcile global quotas asynchronously; a synchronous cross-region check per request is a latency non-starter. Retry storms: all throttled clients retrying at exactly Retry-After re-trip the limit in lockstep - add jitter to the advertised retry time. Observability: per-rule allow/deny rate dashboards catch a fat-fingered rule (limit accidentally set to 0) within seconds of deploy.
hld.cold_prompt: Design a distributed rate limiter for an API gateway fleet: 100k QPS aggregate, 10M active clients, per-client rules like 100 req/min, sub-millisecond decision budget, correct across all gateway nodes. From cold: compare the five algorithms and pick one with justification, show the memory arithmetic for counters vs logs, specify where state lives, make check-and-decrement atomic, define the 429 response contract, and state your failure mode when the backing store dies.
hld.change_request: Mid-design change: enterprise plans now sell global limits of 1M req/min enforced across three regions. Extend your design without adding a cross-region network hop to any request. How do you split, track, and rebalance the quota when one region carries 80% of the traffic?
hld.failure_injection: The Redis cluster backing all counters becomes unreachable for 90 seconds during peak. Choose fail-open or fail-closed, defend it, describe exactly what your backends experience either way, and specify the local fallback that keeps the blast radius acceptable.
tradeoffs: Fail-open vs fail-closed on store outage: fail-open with a conservative in-process fallback limiter - protects user experience while keeping partial backend protection; fail-closed only in front of fragile downstreams.
Sliding-window counter vs sliding-window log: counter - O(1) memory and near-exact (1 GB vs 8 GB at this scale); the log is exact but pays O(rate) memory and work per client.
Token bucket vs fixed window: token bucket when bursts are legitimate traffic; fixed window only when 2x boundary bursts are tolerable in exchange for the cheapest possible implementation.

Central Redis check vs per-node local limits: central - one ~0.3 ms colocated hop buys real accuracy; go local-with-async-reconcile only when the latency budget genuinely forbids the hop, and admit the skew error.
Gateway middleware vs library in every service: gateway - single enforcement point and single config plane; accept that internal service-to-service traffic needs its own limiter story.

qa.q: Write the sliding-window-counter estimate and state its error characteristics.
qa.a: estimate = curr_count + prev_count x (1 - elapsed/window). With limit 100/min, 40% into the current minute, prev=84, curr=36: 36 + 84 x 0.6 = 86.4 -> allow. Error source: it assumes the previous window's requests were uniformly spread; a burst packed at one edge of the previous window makes it slightly over- or under-admit. In production measurements the misclassification rate is a tiny fraction of a percent - acceptable for API limiting.

qa.q: Why must the token-bucket check run as a Redis Lua script, and where does the timestamp come from?
qa.a: The check is a read-modify-write over two values (tokens, last_refill_ts). Two gateways interleaving GET/SET can both observe one remaining token and both admit - over-limit under exactly the load the limiter exists for. A Lua script executes atomically on Redis's single thread, closing the race. Use the Redis TIME command inside the script for the clock so every gateway shares one time source - gateway wall clocks skew and would corrupt refill math.

qa.q: What goes in the 429 response and why add jitter to it?
qa.a: 429 status, Retry-After (seconds until a token is available), and X-RateLimit-Limit/Remaining/Reset so well-behaved SDKs can pace themselves. Jitter the advertised Retry-After: if 10,000 throttled clients all retry at the exact same second, they arrive in lockstep, re-trip the limit, and oscillate - a self-inflicted thundering herd. Randomizing within a small range spreads the retry wave.

hld.title: Chat System (WhatsApp-scale)
hld.worked: REQUIREMENTS. 1:1 chat and groups up to 500 members; delivery states (sent, delivered, read); online presence; offline delivery via mobile push; multi-device sync; message history; media through a separate path. Non-functional: end-to-end delivery p50 under 200 ms, zero message loss (at-least-once plus dedupe), strict per-conversation ordering, WhatsApp scale. ESTIMATION. 1B users; peak concurrent connections ~50M. Messages: 100B/day / 86400 = ~1.2M msg/sec average, ~3M/sec peak. Storage: a text message is ~100 bytes with ids and timestamps -> 100e9 x 100B = 10 TB/day, ~3.6 PB/year -> wide-column store with cold tiering; media never rides the message pipe - it goes to object storage behind a CDN and messages carry URLs. Connections: an epoll/Erlang-class server comfortably holds ~1M mostly idle TCP connections -> 50M concurrent = 50-100 chat servers plus headroom. Bandwidth: 1.2M msg/s x 100B = ~120 MB/s ingress - trivial; the hard problems are connection state, routing, and fan-out, not bytes. API AND PROTOCOL. One persistent WebSocket (or MQTT) per device with ~30 s heartbeats; HTTP only for auth, history pagination, and media via presigned upload URLs. send(conversation_id, client_msg_id, body) -> ack(server_msg_id). client_msg_id is generated on-device and makes retries idempotent - the server dedupes on it. DATA MODEL. messages: partition key conversation_id, clustering key message_id, where message_id is a per-conversation monotonically increasing sequence - one partition scan returns history in order. conversations: id, member list. cursors: (user_id, device_id, conversation_id) -> last acked message_id; this one small table powers both offline delivery and multi-device sync. COMPONENTS. Stateful chat servers holding connections; a session registry (user_id -> device -> chat server, in Redis) for routing; a message service that assigns sequence ids and persists; Kafka for everything async (push notifications, receipts, analytics); a presence service; a media service. FLOW. A sends to chat server 1 -> message service assigns message_id, persists to the conversation partition, acks A (sent) -> looks up B in the session registry -> B online: push down B's connection via B's chat server; B's ack flows back to A as the delivered receipt. B offline: enqueue an APNs/FCM push as a wake-up signal and rely on cursor sync at reconnect - the push is not the delivery mechanism, the store is. Read receipts are ordinary tiny messages on the same pipe. DEEP DIVE: ORDERING, GROUPS, SYNC. Ordering: one sequencer per conversation - practically, partition a Kafka topic or an id-allocator service by conversation_id so a single partition owner assigns gap-free ascending ids. Clients render strictly by message_id, so out-of-order network arrival self-heals, and a gap tells the client exactly which ids to re-request. Group fan-out: store the message ONCE per conversation (shared-conversation model); never copy into 500 member inboxes - that multiplies storage 500x for zero benefit, since ordering already comes from the shared sequence. Fan-out is only the delivery notification: resolve online members via the session registry, group them by chat server, send one batched notification per server. Offline members cost nothing until they sync. Multi-device: every device keeps its own cursors; on reconnect it presents them and the server streams each conversation partition from cursor+1. At-least-once delivery plus client dedupe by message_id yields effective exactly-once. PRESENCE. Heartbeats every ~30 s; two misses -> offline, with a grace period to stop flapping on lossy mobile networks. Never broadcast a flap to all contacts: publish to the presence service and deliver only to users currently viewing that contact (subscribe-on-view) - O(viewers) instead of O(contacts) storms. BOTTLENECKS AND SCALING. Chat server death: 1M clients reconnect at once - exponential backoff with jitter, the LB spreads them across survivors, and cursors make recovery cheap: nothing is lost, clients just re-sync from their cursors. The session registry sits on every message path - cache entries on chat servers and invalidate on reconnect events. Hot groups: batch notifications per target server; if the product later wants 100k-member channels, that tier must flip from push to pull (fetch on open) because per-member notification fan-out stops scaling. Storage hot spots: bucket giant conversations by (conversation_id, day) to cap partition size. Cross-region: users attach to the nearest region; each conversation has a home region that owns its sequencer, and cross-region messages relay through it - per-conversation ordering preserved at the cost of one inter-region hop for remote members.
hld.cold_prompt: Design WhatsApp: 1:1 plus 500-member groups, 100B messages/day, 50M peak concurrent connections, sent/delivered/read receipts, presence, multi-device sync, zero message loss. From cold: protocol choice and why, connection-server count from the arithmetic, session registry and routing, the message ID and ordering scheme, the storage partition layout, offline delivery, group fan-out that does not copy messages 500 times, and the cursor-based sync model.
hld.change_request: Mid-design change: product wants broadcast channels with up to 1M subscribers. Your delivery fan-out was designed for 500-member groups. Rework the channel path - what flips from push to pull, what happens to receipts and unread counts at that scale, and how do small groups stay unaffected?
hld.failure_injection: A chat server holding 1M live connections dies without warning. Walk the next 60 seconds: what do the clients do, what happens in the session registry, which in-flight messages are at risk, and which two design choices (name them) guarantee zero loss and a cheap recovery?
tradeoffs: WebSocket vs long-polling: WebSocket - bidirectional push over one handshake with cheap heartbeats; polling burns battery and adds delivery latency at 100B msgs/day scale.
Shared conversation storage vs per-recipient inbox copies: shared, store once per conversation - group messages must not multiply storage 500x; per-device cursors carry read state instead.

Per-conversation sequence vs global Snowflake IDs: per-conversation monotonic sequence - gap-free ids give trivial ordering, gap detection, and cursor sync; Snowflake alone cannot promise gap-free per-chat order.

At-least-once plus client dedupe vs exactly-once delivery: at-least-once with idempotent client_msg_id - exactly-once across flaky mobile networks is a fiction; dedupe by id is cheap and gives the same observable result.
Presence push-to-all-contacts vs subscribe-on-view: subscribe-on-view - a flapping mobile connection must cost O(current viewers), not O(all contacts), or presence traffic dwarfs message traffic.

qa.q: I open my laptop after a week offline. Exactly how does it catch up on every conversation?
qa.a: Each device stores a cursor per conversation: the last message_id it acked. On connect, the laptop presents its cursors; the server range-scans each conversation partition for message_id > cursor (one ordered partition read in the wide-column store) and streams the results. The device acks, advancing cursors, and dedupes by message_id in case of retry. No scanning of global inboxes, no server-side per-device queues to bloat while offline.

qa.q: Why store a group message once instead of copying it to all 500 members' inboxes?
qa.a: Copying multiplies storage by group size - 100B msgs/day would become tens of PB/day - and buys nothing: ordering already comes from the shared per-conversation sequence, and read state lives in per-device cursors. Delivery only requires notifying online members (a lookup in the session registry, batched per chat server); offline members pull by cursor later. Per-user copies make sense for feed systems where each user's list differs; in chat, every member sees the identical ordered stream.

qa.q: How is a message delivered to a user who is offline, and what role does the mobile push actually play?
qa.a: The message is already durable in the conversation partition before the sender gets the sent ack, so offline delivery is a non-event: the session registry lookup misses, so the system enqueues an APNs/FCM notification with minimal payload. The push is purely a wake-up signal - when the app opens, the device reconnects and cursor-syncs, pulling the real messages from the store. This means lost pushes cannot lose messages, and payload stays out of third-party push infrastructure.

hld.title: News Feed (fan-out on write vs read)
hld.worked: REQUIREMENTS. Publish posts, follow users, read a home feed of followees - reverse-chronological as the baseline with ranking as a later layer. Non-functional: feed read p99 under 200 ms, new posts visible to followers within seconds, 300M DAU, and authors with 100M followers must not degrade anyone. ESTIMATION. Reads: 300M DAU x 5 feed loads/day = 1.5B/day = ~17k QPS average, ~50k peak. Writes: 300M x 0.5 posts/day = 150M posts/day = ~1.7k posts/sec. Average 200 followers -> naive fan-out on write = 1.7k x 200 = ~340k feed-cache inserts/sec - heavy but fine for a worker pool. The killer number: ONE celebrity post to 100M followers = 1e8 inserts; even at 1M inserts/sec that is 100 seconds of pipeline lag for a single post, starving everyone else - this arithmetic alone forces the hybrid design. Storage: feed caches hold post IDs only - 300M users x 500 ids x 8B = ~1.2 TB across a Redis cluster, affordable precisely because bodies are not duplicated. Posts themselves: 150M/day x 1KB = 150 GB/day in the post store. API. POST /posts {content} -> post_id. GET /feed?cursor=last_post_id&limit=20 - cursor pagination, never offset (offsets shift as new posts land, producing duplicates and gaps). POST /follows/{user_id}. DATA MODEL. posts: post_id (Snowflake: 64-bit, timestamp in the high bits, so id order IS time order), author_id, content. follows: indexed in both directions - follower -> followees for the pull path, followee -> followers for the push path. Feed cache: per-user Redis sorted structure of post_ids, capped at ~500-1000 entries. COMPONENTS. Post service persists the post and emits to Kafka. Fanout workers consume, fetch the author's follower list from the graph service in batches, and insert the post_id into each active follower's feed cache. Feed service on read: fetch the user's id list, hydrate bodies from the post cache and authors from the user cache, filter, return one page. DEEP DIVE: PUSH VS PULL AND THE HYBRID. Fan-out on write (push): work happens at post time; a read is O(1) - fetch a precomputed list. Costs: write amplification of followers x posts, wasted pushes to dormant users, and the celebrity melt computed above. Fan-out on read (pull): at read time, fetch recent posts of every followee and merge - zero write amplification and always fresh, but latency scales with followee count and identical merges recompute endlessly for active readers. Hybrid - the right call: classify authors by follower count. Below the threshold (say 100k followers) -> push. Above it -> never fan out; each such author maintains a recent-posts list, and the feed service merges at read time: the precomputed push list plus a pull of recent ids from the handful of mega-authors this user follows. Both inputs are Snowflake-sorted, so it is a cheap k-way merge trimmed to one page. Also skip pushing to users inactive over 30 days and rebuild their feed by pull when they return - most registered accounts are dormant and pushing to them is pure waste. BOTTLENECKS AND SCALING. Celebrity read amplification: one recent-posts key read 50k times/sec -> replicate that key across cache nodes or add per-server in-process caches with 1-2 s TTL; seconds of feed staleness are invisible to users. Deletion: a deleted post_id sits in millions of feed lists - never chase them; filter at hydration time (a deleted post hydrates to null and is dropped from the page). Graph fan-out: a 100M-row follower list must stream through Kafka in batches so one celebrity post becomes thousands of small worker jobs, not one giant one. Feed cache shard loss: it is a cache - fall back to pull on miss, repopulate lazily, and rate-limit the rebuild so the graph and post stores are not stampeded. Ranking later: fetch ~500 candidate ids, score in a model service, reorder, serve - same external API, unchanged architecture, which is exactly why candidates-then-rank is the standard shape. Consistency: eventual everywhere except read-your-own-writes for the author - insert the author's own post into their own feed cache synchronously so they always see it immediately; a follower seeing it 3 seconds late is imperceptible.
hld.cold_prompt: Design a home news feed (Twitter/Instagram style): 300M DAU, 150M posts/day, average 200 followers, celebrities up to 100M followers. From cold: estimation including the write-amplification arithmetic that breaks pure push, the feed API with cursor pagination, the feed-cache data model (ids, not bodies - justify), fan-out on write vs read and your hybrid threshold, the read-path merge and hydration flow, and deletion handling.
hld.change_request: Mid-design change: chronological is dead - a ranking model scores batches of 500 (user, post) pairs in 50 ms. Slot ranking into your architecture: where do candidates come from, what happens to your 200 ms latency budget, what gets cached, and why does your fan-out design barely change?
hld.failure_injection: A Redis shard holding 30M users' precomputed feed lists is lost. What do those users see on their next load, how does the read path degrade gracefully, and how do you rebuild 30M feeds without stampeding the follow graph and post store?
tradeoffs: Fan-out on write vs on read: hybrid - push for authors under ~100k followers, pull for celebrity authors, k-way merge at read; pure push melts on celebrities (1e8 inserts per post), pure pull taxes every read.

Post IDs vs full bodies in feed cache: IDs only - 8 bytes vs ~1 KB is a 100x cache-size difference, and hydration hits an already-hot post cache; duplicating bodies also makes edits/deletes unfixable.

Push to everyone vs skip dormant users: skip users inactive 30+ days and rebuild by pull on return - the majority of registered accounts are dormant, so this slashes fan-out volume for zero visible cost.
Delete propagation vs filter-on-read: filter at hydration - hunting an id through millions of per-user lists is unbounded work; a null-hydrate drop is O(1) per read and immediate.
Offset vs cursor pagination: cursor on last post_id - Snowflake ids are time-sorted so cursors are stable while new posts land; offsets shift and produce duplicates and gaps mid-scroll.

qa.q: Do the arithmetic that proves pure fan-out-on-write cannot survive celebrities.
qa.a: One post by a 100M-follower account = 10^8 feed-cache inserts. A healthy pipeline doing 1M inserts/sec needs 100 seconds for that single post - during which every other author's fan-out queues behind it. Ten celebrity posts an hour and the pipeline is permanently minutes behind. Hence the hybrid: above a follower threshold (~100k) do zero fan-out and let readers pull that author's recent-posts list at read time.

qa.q: Walk the exact read path for one feed page in the hybrid model.
qa.a: 1) Fetch the user's precomputed push list (post ids) from the feed cache. 2) Fetch recent post ids from the recent-posts list of each mega-author the user follows (small, cached, possibly replicated keys). 3) K-way merge by id - Snowflake ids sort by time - and cut at the cursor for 20 items. 4) Hydrate: batch-get bodies from the post cache, authors from the user cache. 5) Drop null hydrations (deleted posts). Total: a handful of cache round trips, no DB on the happy path.

qa.q: Why Snowflake IDs specifically for posts?
qa.a: 64 bits: ~41 bits of millisecond timestamp, ~10 bits machine id, ~12 bits per-ms sequence. Generated locally with zero coordination at any write rate, unique across the fleet, and - the property the feed leans on - numerically sorted order equals time order. That single property enables the k-way merge of push and pull lists and stable cursor pagination without carrying or indexing a separate timestamp.

hld.title: Ride-hailing Location Service (Uber-style geo index)
hld.worked: REQUIREMENTS. Drivers stream GPS while active (every 4 s); riders query nearby available drivers and get matched; live trip tracking and ETA; matching must never double-assign a driver; stale locations must age out on their own. Non-functional: nearby-driver query p99 under 200 ms, write path absorbs enormous update volume, city-level fault isolation. ESTIMATION. 1M concurrently active drivers x 1 update / 4 s = 250k location writes/sec sustained - this single number defines the system. Reads: 20M app opens/day triggering a few nearby queries each, plus 10M ride requests/day -> a few thousand nearby queries/sec, say 5k peak. Writes outnumber reads ~50:1 - optimize the write path. State: 1M drivers x ~50B (id, lat, lng, ts, status, cell) = 50 MB - the entire live index fits in one machine's RAM; the challenge is throughput, hot-spotting, and fault tolerance, never capacity. Trip history: 10M trips/day x ~1000 GPS points x 30B = ~300 GB/day - durable, written asynchronously off the hot path. WHY NOT A REGULAR DATABASE. 250k UPDATEs/sec against a B-tree geospatial index is write-amplification death, and lat/lng range queries need a 2D index anyway. Live location is ephemeral - superseded every 4 s - so durability buys nothing; keep it in memory. GEO INDEX CHOICE. Geohash: base32 prefix strings, simple, but adjacent cells can have wildly different prefixes across boundary lines, so you must compute all 8 neighbors explicitly. Quadtree: adapts depth to density but is an in-memory tree needing rebalancing and is awkward to shard. S2 (Google): Hilbert-curve cells with excellent locality. H3 (Uber's actual system): hexagons - all six neighbors equidistant, no corner-neighbor anomaly, clean fixed resolutions. Right call: H3 (or geohash with explicit neighbor computation) at a resolution where a cell spans ~1-2 km. DATA MODEL AND API. In-memory store (Redis or a purpose-built service): cell_id -> set of driver_ids; driver_id -> {lat, lng, ts, status} with a ~15 s TTL so a driver who stops pinging evaporates automatically. Driver update: compute cell; unchanged -> refresh the record; changed -> SREM from the old cell set, SADD to the new, refresh - O(1) either way. Rider query: compute the rider's cell plus a k-ring of neighbors - a rider near a cell edge has most nearby drivers in adjacent cells, so searching only the home cell is the classic boundary bug - union the driver sets, filter status=available, filter by true distance, rank by road-network ETA; haversine is only the cheap pre-filter because straight-line distance lies near rivers and highways. Transport: gRPC/WebSocket streaming for driver updates - persistent connections make 4 s pings cheap frames rather than full HTTP requests. COMPONENTS. Driver app -> gateway -> location service (sharded in-memory geo index) -> tee to Kafka -> trip-history store (Cassandra) plus ETA/surge/analytics consumers. Rider app -> ride service -> location service query -> dispatch service -> offer to the chosen driver. DEEP DIVE: SHARDING AND MATCHING. Shard the index by geography - a city or contiguous group of H3 cells - via consistent hashing on cell prefix, so one driver update and one rider query each touch exactly one shard. Keep regions large relative to the query radius so cross-shard edge queries are rare, and pad each shard with overlapping border cells for the remainder. Hot cells (downtown rush, a stadium emptying): one cell's set becomes huge and query-heavy -> drop to a finer H3 resolution in dense zones (H3 is natively multi-resolution) or split the cell's set across sub-keys. Replication: each shard has replicas, and because the data is ephemeral, a replacement node rebuilds itself from live driver pings within roughly one 4 s update cycle - recovery is nearly free, which is the payoff for keeping the index non-durable. Matching: dispatch selects candidates and sends an offer; acceptance performs a compare-and-swap on driver status - available -> offered(ride_id) with a lease. Exactly one CAS wins; the loser moves to the next candidate; lease expiry auto-releases a driver stranded by a crashed dispatch flow. No lock service that can orphan locks. BOTTLENECKS AND SCALING. Write pressure: coalesce at the gateway to the newest fix per driver per second under load - intermediate points are worthless for a live map, so dropping them is free fidelity. Surge events: pre-scale shards for known venues; detect hot shards and re-split live. ETA dominates ranking latency -> cache road-distance estimates per (origin cell, destination cell) pair. Trip tracking and analytics consume the Kafka tee, fully isolated from the live query path.
hld.cold_prompt: Design Uber's driver-location service: 1M active drivers pinging every 4 s (250k writes/sec), riders querying nearby available drivers, matching that never double-assigns a driver. From cold: show the estimation and why writes dominate, argue why a conventional DB with a geospatial index fails, compare geohash/quadtree/S2/H3 and pick one, give the in-memory data model with TTL, solve the cell-boundary problem, shard by region, and design double-assignment-proof matching.
hld.change_request: Mid-design change: add surge pricing - real-time supply/demand ratio per neighborhood, refreshed every few seconds, feeding both the pricing engine and driver-repositioning nudges. Extend your pipeline to produce this without adding any load to the hot query path or the in-memory index.
hld.failure_injection: The geo-index shard serving downtown San Francisco crashes at Friday peak - riders there suddenly see zero cars. State the blast radius, what serves reads in the next seconds, how long a replacement takes to become fully accurate, and the specific data property that makes this recovery nearly free.
tradeoffs: In-memory index vs durable DB for live locations: in-memory with ~15 s TTL - each fix is superseded in 4 s so durability buys nothing; persist only trip history, asynchronously via Kafka.
H3 hexagons vs geohash rectangles: H3 - six equidistant neighbors and no boundary-prefix anomaly; geohash is acceptable only if you always compute the 8 neighbors explicitly.

Kafka-first vs index-first for driver updates: write the index directly and tee to Kafka - the live path cannot afford broker latency; history and analytics consume the stream without touching the index.
Haversine vs road-network ETA for ranking: ETA for the final ranking (straight-line lies near rivers and highways), haversine only as the cheap candidate pre-filter; cache ETA per cell-pair.

Distributed lock vs CAS-with-lease for assignment: CAS on driver status with a lease - a crashed dispatcher cannot strand a driver because the lease expires and auto-releases; orphaned locks require janitors.

qa.q: Why must the rider query search a ring of neighboring cells instead of just its own cell?
qa.a: Cells are ~1-2 km wide; a rider standing near a cell edge has most of their genuinely-nearby drivers in adjacent cells, so a single-cell search silently returns a biased, sparse result - the classic boundary bug. A k-ring (home cell plus surrounding ring, 7 cells in H3 for k=1) guarantees radius coverage; then filter candidates by true distance. With geohash this is worse: adjacent cells can differ sharply in prefix across major boundary lines, so the 8 neighbors must be computed explicitly - prefix matching alone is wrong.

qa.q: Trace one driver location update end to end at 250k writes/sec.
qa.a: Driver app sends a frame over its persistent gRPC/WebSocket stream -> gateway (under pressure, coalesce to the newest fix per driver - old points are worthless) -> consistent-hash on cell prefix to one region shard -> in memory: SET driver record {lat, lng, ts, status} with 15 s TTL; if the H3 cell changed, SREM from the old cell's set and SADD to the new. O(1) work, no disk. The update is also teed to Kafka for trip history and surge analytics, keeping those consumers off the live path.

qa.q: Two ride requests arrive simultaneously for the same nearest driver - how is double-assignment prevented?
qa.a: Dispatch performs an atomic compare-and-swap on the driver's status: expect available, set offered(ride_id) with a lease timestamp. Both dispatchers race, exactly one CAS succeeds (single-threaded Redis or a versioned conditional write), and the loser immediately retries with its next candidate. If the winning flow crashes or the driver never responds, the lease expires and the status auto-reverts to available - no distributed lock that a dead process can hold forever.
